EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms were triggered along a nearly stalled back door cold front. Temperatures pushed well into the 80s, with even some 90s, allowing for MLCAPE values of around 2000 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on a power line near the intersection of Northwestern Pike and Sol Shanholtz Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds remained elevated behind an arctic cold front overnight into the early morning hours of the 6th. Temperatures dropped into the single digits, producing wind chills from 12 to 19 degrees below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind chill reading of -14 was recorded in Canaan Heights the morning of the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front crossed eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland the afternoon of the 14th, with snow squalls reducing visibility below one quarter mile at times. Wind gusts over 40 MPH occurred with the snow squalls, and thunder-snow was reported. Behind the front from the morning of the 15th into the 16th, temperatures dropped below zero, with extreme wind chills. The lowest wind chills reported were -37 degrees in Canaan Heights, WV, -33 near Strattanville, PA, -32 at Deep Creek Lake, MD, and -24 at East Palestine, OH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind chill of -24 was recorded the morning of the 15th near Bethany, with a low temperature of -8 on the morning of the 16th.

A complex winter storm moved up the Ohio Valley bringing snow and mixed precipitation from the early morning hours of the 21st into the evening. A heavier band of snow developed in the morning bringing 6 to 7 inches of snow to southern sections of eastern Ohio. Heavy snow fell through the day accumulating 6 to 10 inches across Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, and across Garrett county Maryland. Elsewhere across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania a general 3 to 5 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a very warm and humid air mass the afternoon the the 12th. Scattered severe thunderstorms developed ahead of and along the front across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia. One fatality occurred in Butler county, Pennsylvania when a large tree fell on a mobile home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down on Route 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain occurred within a scenario where cold air damming was taking place across the area, all while relatively warmer moisture aloft in association with a coastal low was progressing across the region. The result was a light rain that froze upon contact and produced ice accretion mainly between a trace and one-tenth of an inch.  Much of the ice accretion was focused along the border with Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accretion across the eastern part of the county averaged one-tenth of an inch. The least amount, only a few hundredths, occurred near the Pocahontas County border. The greatest amount, a little less than two-tenths of an inch, occurred near the Monroe County border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lines of showers and thunderstorms streaked through West Virginia ahead of a large board mid level trough in the upper Midwest. This convection was also hundreds of miles ahead of the surface cold front.  The first line moved through during the early and mid afternoon, while another line of storms moved through during the early evening.  Their movement was toward the northeast at 40 to 45 mph. Gusty winds and downpours were common.  The rain was beneficial for many areas. However, a few storms pulsed briefly to stronger levels during the afternoon storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell at East Lynn Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were blown down.  A large tree fell across Route 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of what was Tropical Storm Bill moved east, through northern Kentucky during the morning hours, reaching the West Virginia border between Huntington and Point Pleasant during the late afternoon.   In the warmer air east of the center, thunderstorms strengthen in central West Virginia during the afternoon and moved north.  A few storms pulsed to severe limits causing some wind damage.||The low pressure center then tracked through central West Virginia during the early evening.  Showers were more persistent over north central West Virginia, compared to southern counties.  One last persistent band of showers, associated just south of the low pressure center, affected the central mountain counties during the evening. ||Rainfall amounts of 1.5 to 2.5 inches occurred.  Yet, the cooperative observer at Clarksburg measured a 24 hour total of 2.86 inches.  Due to the wet ground from rounds of convection during the past week, minor flash flooding occurred during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Muddlety Creek flooded roads.  Water flowed across roads in numerous locations in the northern portion of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated near 45 mph caused tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple cellular convection formed during the early afternoon across eastern Kentucky and southern West Virginia.  A few storms pulsed to stronger levels.||The most public impact appeared to be from a storm that quickly collapsed in the Saint Albans, Nitro, and Cross Lanes vicinity of Kanawha County.  Fallen trees caused around 8,000 customers to be without electricity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down around the Cross Lanes exit of Interstate 64, plus along Goff Mountain Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front passed through the area while an upper-level trough approached from the west. A southerly flow behind the front caused an unstable atmosphere, and moderate amounts of instability developed due to colder air aloft ahead of the approaching trough. There was enough instability for an isolated storm to become severe in the Eastern Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were down along Trough Goad north of Old Stonehouse Road. Trees were estimated to be around three feet in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong east to west front was positioned near the Route 50 corridor.  Thunderstorms formed during the mid and late afternoon and moved east through central West Virginia.  Downpours were most common, with localized amounts of a half inch in 15 minutes.  One storm pulsed to strong levels as it moved out of Ritchie County into Gilmer County.  The same thunderstorm cluster also produced an EF1 tornado in Upshur County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were snapped, twisted, and broken along the path near Queens.  Several buildings sustained roof and property damage.  ||The National Weather Service survey team estimated the strongest wind gusts around 100 mph.

However, a downpour in the city of Beckley caused poor drainage street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was in northern Indiana to central Illinois.  In the warm and muggy air ahead of the front, clusters of thunderstorms formed during the overnight hours.   Huntington had 1 to 1.5 inches of rain in less than an hour.  Outside the city, the airport measured 1.38 inches of rain in an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Urban flash flooding occurred.  The 1st Street viaduct flooded. A vehicle was flooded in the viaduct when it stalled in the deep water.  Other streets in Huntington flooded, including Jackson Avenue and 10th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lines of showers and thunderstorms streaked through West Virginia ahead of a large board mid level trough in the upper Midwest. This convection was also hundreds of miles ahead of the surface cold front.  The first line moved through during the early and mid afternoon, while another line of storms moved through during the early evening.  Their movement was toward the northeast at 40 to 45 mph. Gusty winds and downpours were common.  The rain was beneficial for many areas. However, a few storms pulsed briefly to stronger levels during the afternoon storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to as high as 50 mph blew over swing sets, trampoline, and portable basketball nets and back boards.

Repetitive showers caused 1 to 1.5 inches of rain in about 3 to 4 hours during the predawn. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and moist air mass in advance of a strong late spring cold front ignited scattered thunderstorms across southeast West Virginia. Lightning from one of the storms struck a power pole near Frankford and set it on fire, resulting in power outages for the surrounding area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained storm spotter relayed a report of lightning striking a power pole on U.S. 219 in Frankford.  The lightning set the power pole on fire.  Power was out to a large surrounding area for up to three hours as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms intensified along and east to west front in central West Virginia during the late morning hours.  Downpours were most common across extreme southern Roane County and into Clay County during the late morning. The heaviest rain was concentrated over the Big Sandy Creek drainage.  An automatic rain gauge on Laurel Ridge in Clay County measured 1.28 inches in an hour.  ||A cluster of fast moving thunderstorms moved east across northern Kentucky during the late morning reaching into West Virginia during the afternoon. A mid level disturbance helped maintain another major cluster of showers and thunderstorms during the late night hours.  It moved east, across the same corridor that had been affected with downpours earlier in the day.  This resulted in 3 to 4 rounds of convection within 12 to 14 hours.  The Laurel Ridge rain gauge had around 2.5 inches of rain in 12 hours.||Overall, the rain maximums were on the order of 2 to 2.5 inches in about 12 to 15 hours in central West Virginia.  A strong rise occurred on the lower Elk River, but river levels remained below flood stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second round of flash flooding in less than 15 hours hit Clay County during the late night hours. The heaviest rain was a bit further south in the county, compared to the earlier round on the 10th. ||Route 4 and 16 were both blocked by adjacent small streams.

Upper level disturbances embedded within the flow passed across the forecast area, triggering a few strong to severe thunderstorms. CAPE (Convective Available Potential Energy) values were observed exceeding 2000 J/Kg during the afternoon and early evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last remnants of the mid level disturbance was exiting to the northeast.  Slow moving showers and storms affected central and north central West Virginia during the afternoon and evening.  Local downpours occurred near Clarksburg, with an estimated 1.5 to 2 inches possible between Clarksburg and Salem.  An automatic  gauge in Clarksburg measured 1.2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small creeks overflowed onto roads.  Some of the streams included Murphys Run, Simpson Fork, and Davisson Run.  Water from Simpson Fork got into 2 or 3 homes along Sun Valley Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Headwaters of the Big Sandy Creek flooded, including Cookman Fork along the Roane County Line.   A few of the other flooded small creeks included Camp Run and Valley Fork.  Route 36 or Wallback Road was closed by Valley Fork.  Water rescues and evacuations occurred.  Water surrounded some homes or cut off access to them.  One young male adult fell into a flooded stream while rescuing his dogs.  He managed to survive uninjured, after being pushed a few hundred yards downstream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stationary front extended across PA through the period. Pre frontal showers increased moisture over portions of Eastern WV. This combined with clearing skies and weakening winds as high pressure built in led to the formation of dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities of one quarter mile or less was reported at W99.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and light winds with high pressure building in from the south combined with lingering moisture from showers the previous evening to result in patchy dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities of one quarter mile or less were reported at CBE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually deep low for late June moved through the Ohio Valley with a slow-moving front triggering scattered to numerous thunderstorms across southeast West Virginia. Easterly flow into the mountains enhanced the upslope component of rainfall and high precipitable waters made for some very efficient rainfall processes. Radar estimates of rainfall showed 2.5 to 3 inches, with isolated 4-inch amounts that fell in about 3 to 4 hours along an east-west axis near Alvon, mainly in the Anthony Creek basin.  Nearby IFLOWS gages at Alvon (ALVW2) and Blue Bend (HOPW2) measured 2.5 to 3 inches in about 4 hours ending at about 3 AM LST.  In addition to the one known flash flood event with this system, wet soils from the heavy rain caused several trees to uproot, blocking a few of the local roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unnamed tributary to Anthony Creek near Alvon flooded portions of Route 92 as a drainage culvert became completely blocked by large logs and other debris. The rerouted water undercut a portion of the road bed of Route 92 causing significant damage to the road. Water was reportedly up to 5 feet deep along portions of the road and a nearby restaurant had high water up to the same depth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed over the upper Ohio Valley during the day on May 31st ahead of surface low pressure that would cross the region in the evening. With moderate instability and shear, several storms produced severe wind damage. In addition, where shear was enhanced near a stationary boundary, a brief EF-0 tornado touched down in Hermitage, Mercer county Pennsylvania. Damage occurred at and around the Sheetz in Hermitage and a truck was flipped onto it's side in the parking lot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported trees down around the city of Fairmont including one on Palatine Ave and Moyer way.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, well defined mid level circulation lifted northeast into eastern Kentucky by late afternoon.  Ahead of this disturbance, cellular convection formed and moved north into southern West Virginia.  New convection formed up the mountain counties, reaching into Upshur County.  Local downpours of .5 to .75 inches of rain in 15 minutes, and 1 to 2 inches of rain in less than 2 hours occurred.  The most widespread area of heavy rain occurred over the Laurel Fork and Clear Fork basins in northern Wyoming County.  Most of the flash flooding was minor and occurred along the small runs and feeder streams.  Water flowed across and blocked roads.  The larger creeks and headwater rivers remained within their banks.||The most persistent showers formed along the border of McDowell County with Mercer County and Tazewell County in Virginia. The heaviest rain, upwards of 4 inches, remained in Mercer County, with only minor flash flooding in McDowell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams near the Boone County line flooded, including Garrett Fork.

Damage occurred at and around the Sheetz in Hermitage and a truck was flipped onto it's side in the parking lot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported a tree down on Sugar Lane near Rivesville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported partial roof damage to a Napa Auto Parts building on route 7 in Sabraton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, well defined mid level circulation lifted northeast into eastern Kentucky by late afternoon.  Ahead of this disturbance, cellular convection formed and moved north into southern West Virginia.  New convection formed up the mountain counties, reaching into Upshur County.  Local downpours of .5 to .75 inches of rain in 15 minutes, and 1 to 2 inches of rain in less than 2 hours occurred.  The most widespread area of heavy rain occurred over the Laurel Fork and Clear Fork basins in northern Wyoming County.  Most of the flash flooding was minor and occurred along the small runs and feeder streams.  Water flowed across and blocked roads.  The larger creeks and headwater rivers remained within their banks.||The most persistent showers formed along the border of McDowell County with Mercer County and Tazewell County in Virginia. The heaviest rain, upwards of 4 inches, remained in Mercer County, with only minor flash flooding in McDowell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flash flooding occurred near the county line.  Route 52 was blocked by a rock and debris slide near Justice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms intensified along and east to west front in central West Virginia during the late morning hours.  Downpours were most common across extreme southern Roane County and into Clay County during the late morning. The heaviest rain was concentrated over the Big Sandy Creek drainage.  An automatic rain gauge on Laurel Ridge in Clay County measured 1.28 inches in an hour.  ||A cluster of fast moving thunderstorms moved east across northern Kentucky during the late morning reaching into West Virginia during the afternoon. A mid level disturbance helped maintain another major cluster of showers and thunderstorms during the late night hours.  It moved east, across the same corridor that had been affected with downpours earlier in the day.  This resulted in 3 to 4 rounds of convection within 12 to 14 hours.  The Laurel Ridge rain gauge had around 2.5 inches of rain in 12 hours.||Overall, the rain maximums were on the order of 2 to 2.5 inches in about 12 to 15 hours in central West Virginia.  A strong rise occurred on the lower Elk River, but river levels remained below flood stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams flooded and closed roads in the extreme southern portion of the county, mainly south of the interstate, and along the Clay County border. This included along such small streams as Granny Creek, Pigeon Run, and Little Pigeon Run. As the smaller streams and runs drained, flash flooding occurred along Big Sandy Creek around Amma and Osbornes Mill. Water surrounded some homes or cut off access to them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of the 26th, a low pressure system was organizing over the lower Ohio River.  Meanwhile, ahead of that system, a frontal boundary stretched east through southern Ohio and central West Virginia.  ||Thunderstorms formed along the frontal boundary in southern Ohio, with more widespread rain north of the front.  Those thunderstorms moved slightly south of east into western West Virginia. One of the leading storms was severe with mid level rotation.  It pulsed to its strongest level near Ripley of Jackson County. This caused widespread tree damage from Ripley east along Route 33.  One brief EF0 was also declared from a storm survey.  The strongest winds were estimated with the downburst winds rather than the tornado.||Repetitive thunderstorms and showers affected northern Mason County and central Jackson County.  Downpours occurred.  An automatic gauge at Mason in Mason County measured 1.88 in the hour from 1730 to 1830E.  Its total in 3 hours was 2.55 inches.  The Ripley water plant measured 2.23 inches, most of which fell in a hour.||One location along the headwaters of Stationcamp Run along Route 5/13 saw large hail, followed by severe wind gusts. The downpour then caused flash flooding in the same location.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees fell, causing power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level disturbance helped trigger 1 cluster of storms during the predawn hours of the 18th.  Those storms moved east, through north central West Virginia.  Other storms affected the Interstate 64 corridor later that evening.  Two small separate areas in Putnam County had the heaviest rain, causing localized flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Harmons Creek flooded and damaged roads.  Water got inside a few buildings.  One local couple's vehicle stalled in the rising water. Neighbors pulled their vehicle to safety using an ATV.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level disturbance helped trigger 1 cluster of storms during the predawn hours of the 18th.  Those storms moved east, through north central West Virginia.  Other storms affected the Interstate 64 corridor later that evening.  Two small separate areas in Putnam County had the heaviest rain, causing localized flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trace Fork flooded and closed roads.  Since the heavy rain was more localized, the gauge further down Hurricane Creek did not rise as high as the previous event.  Water levels near the gauge rose less than 7 feet and crested below 12 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure wave moved east during the day, from northern Ohio to along the border of Pennsylvania and New York.  Its associated cold front sagged southwest and approached western West Virginia during the afternoon.  Dew points were around 70 degrees ahead of the front.  Short lines of showers and thunderstorms formed.  One larger cluster of storms moved out of eastern Kentucky during the late afternoon.  That convection reached the southern coal fields of West Virginia.||The main result was the loss of electricity from trees falling on power lines.  The utility company reported about 3,000 customers in Logan County, plus around 1700 in Mingo County, were without electricity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure wave moved east during the day, from northern Ohio to along the border of Pennsylvania and New York.  Its associated cold front sagged southwest and approached western West Virginia during the afternoon.  Dew points were around 70 degrees ahead of the front. That convection reached the southern coal fields of West Virginia.||The main result was the loss of electricity from trees falling on power lines.  The utility company reported about 3,000 customers in Logan County, plus around 1700 in Mingo County, were without electricity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms intensified along and east to west front in central West Virginia during the late morning hours.  Downpours were most common across extreme southern Roane County and into Clay County during the late morning. The heaviest rain was concentrated over the Big Sandy Creek drainage.  An automatic rain gauge on Laurel Ridge in Clay County measured 1.28 inches in an hour.  ||A cluster of fast moving thunderstorms moved east across northern Kentucky during the late morning reaching into West Virginia during the afternoon. A mid level disturbance helped maintain another major cluster of showers and thunderstorms during the late night hours.  It moved east, across the same corridor that had been affected with downpours earlier in the day.  This resulted in 3 to 4 rounds of convection within 12 to 14 hours.  The Laurel Ridge rain gauge had around 2.5 inches of rain in 12 hours.||Overall, the rain maximums were on the order of 2 to 2.5 inches in about 12 to 15 hours in central West Virginia.  A strong rise occurred on the lower Elk River, but river levels remained below flood stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: School House Road and Sharon Road were closed by flood waters from Jerry Run.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, well defined mid level circulation lifted northeast into eastern Kentucky by late afternoon.  Ahead of this disturbance, cellular convection formed and moved north into southern West Virginia.  New convection formed up the mountain counties, reaching into Upshur County.  Local downpours of .5 to .75 inches of rain in 15 minutes, and 1 to 2 inches of rain in less than 2 hours occurred.  The most widespread area of heavy rain occurred over the Laurel Fork and Clear Fork basins in northern Wyoming County.  Most of the flash flooding was minor and occurred along the small runs and feeder streams.  Water flowed across and blocked roads.  The larger creeks and headwater rivers remained within their banks.||The most persistent showers formed along the border of McDowell County with Mercer County and Tazewell County in Virginia. The heaviest rain, upwards of 4 inches, remained in Mercer County, with only minor flash flooding in McDowell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams near the Logan County line flooded, making roads impassable.  This included Meadow Fork and Missouri Branch.  A 5 yard section of the small road along Missouri Branch was washed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short lines of showers and thunderstorms formed in the humid air during the afternoon.  This was well ahead of a cold front in northwest Ohio to southern portions of Indiana and Illinois. ||The front sank south into southern Ohio early on the 8th.  Repetitive showers caused 1 to 1.5 inches of rain in about 3 to 4 hours during the predawn.  This band of heavier rain was mostly from Cabell County on east and north through Putnam, northern Kanawha, southern Jackson, Roane, Calhoun, Braxton. northern Webster, southern Lewis, and Upshur Counties.||After a lull in the rain during most of the day, another round of showers formed south of the stalled front in southern Ohio and northern West Virginia.  Rain totals of .5 to an inch were enough to cause minor flash flooding in Roane County during the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down at intersection of Heizer Creek Road and Route 62.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure pushed to the north and west, with the associated warm front slowly approaching from the south. High pressure to the north kept a wedge of cold air in place, which resulted in freezing rain as precipitation overspread ahead of the warm front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center in Martinsburg relayed a report of 0.01 inch ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked across the Mid Atlantic. As the low pushed offshore and deepened while the upper level trough became negatively tilted, precipitation became more widespread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm snow totals between 3.0 and 4.5 inches were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spotty thunderstorms developed with afternoon heating across portions of the central Appalachians.  Most of the thunderstorms were weak due to the lack of any upper level support.  However, cloud-to-ground lightning was observed which, in one case, struck a home, causing minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house near the intersection of Anderson and Stonehouse Roads, causing minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked across the Mid Atlantic. As the low pushed offshore and deepened while the upper level trough became negatively tilted, precipitation became more widespread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm snow total of 3.5 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms associated with a larger cluster of storms advanced southeast through southeast West Virginia. One section of these storms contained damaging wind that brought trees down over a large swath of Mercer County. Another, smaller, section of this line of storms downed three trees in Monroe County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large number of trees were blown down across Mercer County. Communities that experienced tree and tree limb damage include Matoka, Montcalm, Spanishburg, Bluefield, Athens, and Princeton. A tree limb fell on a moving vehicle near Bluefield. There were no known injuries. Damage values are estimated.

One section of these storms contained damaging wind that brought trees down over a large swath of Mercer County. Another, smaller, section of this line of storms downed three trees in Monroe County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind blew tree trees down. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved south through the region. Southerly flow ahead of the front led to an unstable air mass, which combined with steepening mid level lapse rates and increased sheer led to numerous severe thunderstorms being triggered right ahead of the front.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down at the 500 block of Christian Church Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary acted as the triggering point for an initial line of showers and thunderstorms, with many of the storms becoming severe with strong moisture advection combining with diurnal heating and leading to an unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were reported down on U.S. 50 near the Dolan Dr intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture ahead of an approaching cold front led to valley dense fog development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities of one quarter mile or less were reported at CBE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure building over the region weakened winds, which combined with increased moisture and clearing skies led to short-lived valley dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities of one quarter mile or less were reported at W99.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light southeast flow developed over the region overnight as weak low pressure pushed to the northwest, injecting in low level moisture. As temperatures dropped with clearing skies, areas of dense fog developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interpolated between CBE and OKV which reported visibilities one quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms intensified during the late afternoon in central and southern Ohio, as a weak low pressure was moving across northern Ohio.  A humid air mass existed in the mid Ohio Valley with surface dew points in the lower 70s.  The storms moved into West Virginnia during the evening hours.  A few locations got over 2 inches of rain, but the ground was relatively dry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported 2.15 inches of rain in a few hours.  One home had water enter inside, due to a blocked culvert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lines of showers and thunderstorms developed in advance of an approaching cold front. Some of these storms contained damaging winds that downed trees, and one wind gust snapped a power pole.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped a power pole in half in the community of Fairlea. . Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lines of showers and thunderstorms developed in advance of an approaching cold front. Some of these storms contained damaging winds that downed trees, and one wind gust snapped a power pole.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down multiple trees along Route 10 in Matoka. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lines of showers and thunderstorms developed in advance of an approaching cold front. Some of these storms contained damaging winds that downed trees, and one wind gust snapped a power pole.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down multiple trees along Route 19 in the Spanishburg area. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front the afternoon of August 3rd into the early morning hours of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking just offshore of Delmarva brought a mix of precipitation to the area as a result of cold air wedging from a retreating high pressure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported between a trace and a tenth of an inch of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking just offshore of Delmarva brought a mix of precipitation to the area as a result of cold air wedging from a retreating high pressure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Cherry Grove reported 0.25 inches of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly approached and moved south through the Mid Atlantic late in the day. Ahead of the front, southerly flow ushered in a warm and moist air mass. Numerous showers and thunderstorms with embedded strong to severe storms formed to the southwest and progressed to the northeast prior to the cold frontal passage. This activity produced copious amounts rain and led to flash flooding across the eastern panhandle of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Archer Road in Middleway was flooded with about six to eight inches of flowing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air remained wedged over the area as high pressure retreated to the north. Strong southerly flow ahead of a cold front injected in warmer air aloft, resulting in widespread mixed precipitation. Freezing rain was noted mainly at higher elevations, with sleet and rain at the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trace of ice was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air remained wedged over the area as high pressure retreated to the north. Strong southerly flow ahead of a cold front injected in warmer air aloft, resulting in widespread mixed precipitation. Freezing rain was noted mainly at higher elevations, with sleet and rain at the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported 0.05 inches of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers fell on either side of an arctic cold front.  Snow accumulations of 3 to 6 inches were common during the day and into the evening of the 12th across Randolph, Webster, and Pocahontas Counties.  Lesser amounts fell elsewhere.  Winds increased into the 15 to 25 mph range during the afternoon and evening, as the snow showers diminished to flurries.  Wind chill readings dropped into the minus 10 to minus 15 degree range during the overnight periods across these central mountain counties.  With clearing before dawn on the 13th, the actual air temperatures fell mostly into the zero to 5 below zero range.  However, Elkins did reach 6 below zero.  On the mountaintop at Snowshoe the temperature fell to 10 below zero.  ||Some schools were delayed or canceled.

Another arctic front swept through during the afternoon of the 14th.  Temperatures dropped from the upper 20s and 30s into the teens in a few hours.  In the wake of the front, wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph were common well into the night.  Some power outages occurred. ||A burst of snow occurred along the front.  Accumulations were mostly 2 inches or less.  However, the snow showers were a bit more persistent across the central mountain counties on south into the Wyoming and McDowell Counties of the southern coal fields.  Here accumulations of 3 to 4 inches were common.  A few spots in the central mountains, around Richwood and Snowshoe had 6 inches of snow accumulation in 12 to 18 hours.||By dawn on the 15th, temperatures dropped into the single digits in the lowlands with a few readings just below zero in the north central counties.  In the mountain counties, the coldest temperature around dawn on the 15th was 15 below zero at Snowshoe.  Bartow observed minus 7.     ||Early on the 15th, wind chill readings of minus 10 to minus 15 were common, but dropped into the minus 20 to minus 30 range over the mountain counties. Despite sunshine on the 15th, temperatures over the central mountains remained on either side of zero degrees, based on elevation.  The clear sky and light winds during the evening of the 15th allowed temperatures to drop below zero in many valley locations over some northern counties.  Before clouds started to increase during the predawn of the 16th, Elkins dropped to 12 below zero, Frost was minus 5, Clarksburg airport was minus 4, Rock Cave was minus 3 and West Union was 2 below zero.

Low pressure passed just to the south of the region, with overspreading precipitation pushing to the north. High pressure to the north resulting in cold air wedging led to the precipitation falling as freezing drizzle and rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported between a trace and 0.13 inches of ice.

Bitter cold Arctic high pressure brought temperatures well below zero the morning of the 20th, with many low temperature records broken.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low of -10 was recorded at Terra Alta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked across the Mid Atlantic. As the low pushed offshore and deepened while the upper level trough became negatively tilted, precipitation became more widespread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm snow totals between 2.0 and 4.0 inches were reported.

A fast moving clipper system moved through on the 6th.  It was aided by a wind speed maximum in the flow aloft.  Surface temperatures were only in the upper teens to lower 20s.||Four to 6 inches of dry snow accumulated in 12 to 15 hours over a portion of the lowland counties in West Virginia.  This was mainly along the Route 50 corridor.  In the more mountainous counties of Barbour, Upshur, and Randolph, snow accumulations of 6 to 8 inches were common.  The maximum snow accumulation reported was near 10 inches from Alpena.  The water content of the snow was mostly 0.25 to 0.40 inches.||This was the first accumulating snow storm of the winter for the northern lowland counties. Further south in West Virginia, the snow amounts were much less, diminishing to zero in the Huntington to Charleston corridor.||The higher snow criteria in north central West Virginia for heavy snow, compared to the mid Ohio Valley, was the determining factor for 2 different event categories in this episode, despite rather similar accumulations.

Low pressure pushed to the north and west, with the associated warm front slowly approaching from the south. High pressure to the north kept a wedge of cold air in place, which resulted in freezing rain as precipitation overspread ahead of the warm front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surrounding counties reported between a trace and a couple hundredths of an inch of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure pushed to the north and west, with the associated warm front slowly approaching from the south. High pressure to the north kept a wedge of cold air in place, which resulted in freezing rain as precipitation overspread ahead of the warm front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trace of ice was reported in Rig.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In less than a week, a second arctic front swept through West Virginia during the early afternoon hours of the 18th.  Snow showers formed ahead of the front. Bands of snow showers lingered into the evening over the central mountains and southern lowland counties as temperatures dropped into the single digits before midnight.  Snow accumulations of 2 to 3 inches were common around Huntington on through the southern coal field counties and into the central mountains.||Temperatures dropped into the zero to 5 below range just after dawn on the 19th for most areas.  Snowshoe reached minus 14.  Despite sunshine through icy low clouds, daytime readings only recovered into the 5 to 10 degree range in the lowlands.  Temperatures remained below zero during the daylight hours of the 19th across the high mountain terrain. The daytime high temperature on the 19th at Snowshoe was minus 11.  ||Wind chill readings of minus 10 to minus 20 were felt in the lowlands during the day on the 19th, while minus 20 to minus 30 was endured across the mountain counties. ||The diminishing winds and a clear sky developed first over southern counties of the state, then moved north during the overnight hours of the 19th into the 20th. With a fresh deep snow pack, temperatures dropped well below zero for dawn on Friday, the 20th.  The coldest official temperature was minus 24 at Mt Nebo of Nicholas County.  Along Lockhart Fork near Sandyville in Jackson County, 23 below zero was measured. Other official temperatures included minus 21 at Sissonville, minus 19 at East Lynn in Wayne County, minus 18 at Sutton and Snowshoe, minus 17 at the National Weather Service office at Southridge, minus 16 at Grantsville, minus 16 at Huntington, Spencer, Saint Albans, and West Union.  The airport near Pineville observed minus 18.  Charleston dropped to minus 11.  An unofficial temperature of 16 below zero was observed in Hurricane of Putnam County.   The southern plateau was not quite as bitter. Beckley had 9 below zero for its minimum temperature.  Readings of minus 5 to minus 10 were common across north central counties. For example, downtown Clarksburg had minus 6, while the nearby airport had minus 7. | |In several counties, the morning of Friday the 20th was the coldest since the cold waves of February 1996 and January 1994.  For example, the minus 17 at Huntington was the coldest since the minus 21 degrees back in January 1994.  The minus 11 at Charleston was the coldest since the minus 12 in February of 1996.  ||Two deaths of young adults in the Smokehouse Fork area of Logan County were indirectly related to the cold temperatures.  State police listed the cold as a contributing factor. ||At one point near dawn on the 20th, about 10,000 customers were without power in Kanawha County.  Emergency warming shelters were set up by churches and towns.  Several water lines broke due to the cold and continued for a few days as temperatures moderated. Some of the broken water pipes were underground, while others were inside buildings. One example was under a street in the East End of Charleston.  Water lines also broke inside a Marshall dormitory, and in the Boone County Courthouse in Madison.  Water collected 1 to 2 feet in a portion of that court house basement, damaging the flooring.  Several storage tanks for a water utility company drained due to broken pipes.  This cut off water service to thousands in the Charleston vicinity and in Boone County.  Some residents were without water for several days.

The daytime high temperature on the 19th at Snowshoe was minus 11.  ||Wind chill readings of minus 10 to minus 20 were felt in the lowlands during the day on the 19th, while minus 20 to minus 30 was endured across the mountain counties. ||The diminishing winds and a clear sky developed first over southern counties of the state, then moved north during the overnight hours of the 19th into the 20th. With a fresh deep snow pack, temperatures dropped well below zero for dawn on Friday, the 20th.  The coldest official temperature was minus 24 at Mt Nebo of Nicholas County.  Along Lockhart Fork near Sandyville in Jackson County, 23 below zero was measured. Other official temperatures included minus 21 at Sissonville, minus 19 at East Lynn in Wayne County, minus 18 at Sutton and Snowshoe, minus 17 at the National Weather Service office at Southridge, minus 16 at Grantsville, minus 16 at Huntington, Spencer, Saint Albans, and West Union.  The airport near Pineville observed minus 18.  Charleston dropped to minus 11.  An unofficial temperature of 16 below zero was observed in Hurricane of Putnam County.   The southern plateau was not quite as bitter. Beckley had 9 below zero for its minimum temperature.  Readings of minus 5 to minus 10 were common across north central counties. For example, downtown Clarksburg had minus 6, while the nearby airport had minus 7. | |In several counties, the morning of Friday the 20th was the coldest since the cold waves of February 1996 and January 1994.  For example, the minus 17 at Huntington was the coldest since the minus 21 degrees back in January 1994.  The minus 11 at Charleston was the coldest since the minus 12 in February of 1996.  ||Two deaths of young adults in the Smokehouse Fork area of Logan County were indirectly related to the cold temperatures.  State police listed the cold as a contributing factor. ||At one point near dawn on the 20th, about 10,000 customers were without power in Kanawha County.  Emergency warming shelters were set up by churches and towns.  Several water lines broke due to the cold and continued for a few days as temperatures moderated. Some of the broken water pipes were underground, while others were inside buildings. One example was under a street in the East End of Charleston.  Water lines also broke inside a Marshall dormitory, and in the Boone County Courthouse in Madison.  Water collected 1 to 2 feet in a portion of that court house basement, damaging the flooring.  Several storage tanks for a water utility company drained due to broken pipes.  This cut off water service to thousands in the Charleston vicinity and in Boone County.  Some residents were without water for several days.

A fast moving low pressure system, dropping southward out of the northern plains, brought snow to the region during the day on the 6th. Two to four inches of snow were reported across much of the area south of I-70, with five to eight inches reported in the higher elevations in Tucker county, WV, where up-slope enhancement and better moisture were present for efficient snowfall.

Along Lockhart Fork near Sandyville in Jackson County, 23 below zero was measured. Other official temperatures included minus 21 at Sissonville, minus 19 at East Lynn in Wayne County, minus 18 at Sutton and Snowshoe, minus 17 at the National Weather Service office at Southridge, minus 16 at Grantsville, minus 16 at Huntington, Spencer, Saint Albans, and West Union.  The airport near Pineville observed minus 18.  Charleston dropped to minus 11.  An unofficial temperature of 16 below zero was observed in Hurricane of Putnam County.   The southern plateau was not quite as bitter. Beckley had 9 below zero for its minimum temperature.  Readings of minus 5 to minus 10 were common across north central counties. Several storage tanks for a water utility company drained due to broken pipes.  This cut off water service to thousands in the Charleston vicinity and in Boone County.  Some residents were without water for several days.

Another arctic front swept through during the afternoon of the 14th.  Temperatures dropped from the upper 20s and 30s into the teens in a few hours.  In the wake of the front, wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph were common well into the night.  Some power outages occurred. ||A burst of snow occurred along the front.  Accumulations were mostly 2 inches or less.  However, the snow showers were a bit more persistent across the central mountain counties on south into the Wyoming and McDowell Counties of the southern coal fields.  Here accumulations of 3 to 4 inches were common.  A few spots in the central mountains, around Richwood and Snowshoe had 6 inches of snow accumulation in 12 to 18 hours.||By dawn on the 15th, temperatures dropped into the single digits in the lowlands with a few readings just below zero in the north central counties.  In the mountain counties, the coldest temperature around dawn on the 15th was 15 below zero at Snowshoe.  Bartow observed minus 7.     ||Early on the 15th, wind chill readings of minus 10 to minus 15 were common, but dropped into the minus 20 to minus 30 range over the mountain counties. Despite sunshine on the 15th, temperatures over the central mountains remained on either side of zero degrees, based on elevation.  The clear sky and light winds during the evening of the 15th allowed temperatures to drop below zero in many valley locations over some northern counties.  Before clouds started to increase during the predawn of the 16th, Elkins dropped to 12 below zero, Frost was minus 5, Clarksburg airport was minus 4, Rock Cave was minus 3 and West Union was 2 below zero.

A fast moving clipper system moved through on the 6th.  It was aided by a wind speed maximum in the flow aloft.  Surface temperatures were only in the upper teens to lower 20s.||Four to 6 inches of dry snow accumulated in 12 to 15 hours over a portion of the lowland counties in West Virginia.  This was mainly along the Route 50 corridor.  In the more mountainous counties of Barbour, Upshur, and Randolph, snow accumulations of 6 to 8 inches were common.  The maximum snow accumulation reported was near 10 inches from Alpena.  The water content of the snow was mostly 0.25 to 0.40 inches.||This was the first accumulating snow storm of the winter for the northern lowland counties. Further south in West Virginia, the snow amounts were much less, diminishing to zero in the Huntington to Charleston corridor.||The higher snow criteria in north central West Virginia for heavy snow, compared to the mid Ohio Valley, was the determining factor for 2 different event categories in this episode, despite rather similar accumulations.

Rounds of showers and thunderstorms occurred on the 3rd.  A frontal boundary extended east from a weak low pressure moving up the Ohio River Valley. The low pressure crossed into West Virginia early on the 4th.   Local small downpours occurred with 1 to 2 inches of rain.  No serious flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the night, a car with 4 occupants attempted to drive through a natural stream crossing off of Route 19 near Harts.  A few mobile homes were located on the opposite side. The stream was Big Harts Creek.  The stream was swollen, but still well within its bank. No flooding was occurring.||The car got stuck in the stream bed.  Everyone made it to safety.  State Police said 2 individuals went back to the car to get some items out.  Both were swept away by the water. One was rescued. The body of a 48 year old female was found after dawn, about a mile down stream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Surface low pressure system, supported by a strong shortwave aloft, moved from the upper Midwest toward Pennsylvania by the afternoon on the 9th. Some of these storms became severe and a small bowing line developed, which brought down trees across southwestern Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported wires down in Morgantown.

After the arctic deep freeze at dawn on the 20th, snow, sleet, and freezing rain spread over West Virginia between 0300E and 0600E on the 21st. Luckily, it was a Saturday, and travel was limited.  ||A widespread variation of snow totals resulted.  A southeast wind near the ground kept the eastern slopes colder.  This resulted in mostly heavy wet snow, before diminishing and ending as some drizzle during the evening hours.  Snow accumulations of 10 to 20 inches occurred.  For example, Beckley had a 20 inch snowfall in about 15 hours.  The liquid equivalent was 2.10 inches. Snowshoe had a 24 inches snowfall. Their snow depth at Snowshoe went from13 inches prior to the storm, to 35 inches.  At Buckeye on the Greenbrier River, the snow depth went from 8 inches prior to the storm, to 26 inches.  Meanwhile, in northern Pocahontas County at Bartow, their snow depth increased from 7 inches before the storm, to 22 inches.  Roads became impassible between Valley Head and Snowshoe during the afternoon hours.||In the Oak Hill, Mount Hope, and Fayetteville region, accumulations of 10 to 14 inches were seen.  In the Calvin and Craigsville area of Nicholas County 14 to 17 inches of snow was reported by spotters.   Accumulations dropped off into the 6 to 8 inch range in the Tygart River Valley around Elkins and Beverly.  Wyoming and McDowell Counties even saw heavy snow with Ocean reporting 10 inches.  A spotter in McGraws reported 15 inches of snow.  The department of highways storage shed for salt at its Raleigh County headquarters had its roof collapse due to the wet of the heavy snow.  A service station canopy in Beaver also collapsed.  A carport in Simon in Wyoming County collapsed and damaged the parked car.  Snow rolled off of Bolt Mountain and blocked Route 99 to a depth of 15 to 20 feet.         ||Along the Ohio River from Point Pleasant on up the river, the precipitation also stayed mostly as wet snow, but not as heavy.  This included Ravenswood, Parkersburg, Pennsboro, St Marys, and Middlebourne.  In this vicinity, snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were common.  A few 8 inch accumulations were received from southern Wood County and in Ritchie County. ||Meanwhile, between these 2 distinct regions, a milder wedge of air drove northeast during the mid and late morning hours, from southwestern lowlands.  This included areas near Williamson, Logan, and Huntington, on northeast through Charleston.  This milder air quickly reached Glenville, Sutton, Weston, and Grafton.  Snow accumulations here were mostly less than 2 inches, as the initial snow changed to freezing rain.  The freezing rain lasted 3 to 4 hours during the morning. The cold ground temperatures allowed freezing rain to continue even with air temperatures of 33 and 34 degrees.  The ice accumulated to a quarter of an inch.  Vehicles were encased in a layer of ice.  Over 2,000 customers lost electricity in Kanawha County, around 1,000 in Wayne and nearly 700 in Cabell County. The freezing rain became mostly rain by midday, with some melting occurring during the afternoon.  ||The rain was heaviest over the southern coal field counties, around Logan and Mingo Counties. The liquid total was over 2 inches.  Melting slush and snow piles from plowing and shoveling prevented the normal drainage of water. Water pooled on many roads.  Ice filled streams were swollen.  Minor flooding occurred around Champmanville, Big Creek, and Shively during the evening.  Water surrounded some mobile homes and blocked low spots on roads.  No significant damage was reported.  Chunks of ice were left when the water receded. Larger streams, such as Twelvepole Creek in Wayne County ran high into Sunday the 22nd.  Ice dams in residential gutters and downspouts allowed some runoff to seep into homes.

Low pressure passed just to the south of the region, with overspreading precipitation pushing to the north. High pressure to the north resulting in cold air wedging led to the precipitation falling as freezing drizzle and rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported between a trace and 0.05 inches of ice.

Bitter cold Arctic high pressure brought temperatures well below zero the morning of the 20th, with many low temperature records broken.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low temperature of -8 was recorded near Fairmont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Steady rain began during the morning on the 10th.  The rain was associated with a warm front.  The rain increased during the late afternoon and evening hours over north central counties.  Rainfall totals were beginning to exceed an inch.  Several small streams were still running well above normal before the rain even began.||Minor flooding along small streams occurred in the usual flood prone areas along roads during the morning hours on the 11th.  The final rainfall total by early morning was 1 to 1.5 inches in north central counties into the central mountains.||A strong rise occurred on headwater rivers overnight into the 11th.  With milder temperatures and more snow melt east of Cheat Mountain compared to the previous week, the upper Greenbrier River caused minor flooding around Marlinton on the 11th.|Other minor river flooding occurred along the Tygart Valley River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several small streams caused minor flooding of roads.  One example was Shinns Run near Shinnston.  The strong rise on the West Fork also caused some minor water problems, but the river crested under stage.

The liquid total was over 2 inches.  Melting slush and snow piles from plowing and shoveling prevented the normal drainage of water. Water pooled on many roads.  Ice filled streams were swollen.  Minor flooding occurred around Champmanville, Big Creek, and Shively during the evening.  Water surrounded some mobile homes and blocked low spots on roads.  No significant damage was reported.  Chunks of ice were left when the water receded. Larger streams, such as Twelvepole Creek in Wayne County ran high into Sunday the 22nd.  Ice dams in residential gutters and downspouts allowed some runoff to seep into homes.

The liquid total was over 2 inches.  Melting slush and snow piles from plowing and shoveling prevented the normal drainage of water. Water pooled on many roads.  Ice filled streams were swollen.  Minor flooding occurred around Champmanville, Big Creek, and Shively during the evening.  Water surrounded some mobile homes and blocked low spots on roads.  No significant damage was reported.  Chunks of ice were left when the water receded. Larger streams, such as Twelvepole Creek in Wayne County ran high into Sunday the 22nd.  Ice dams in residential gutters and downspouts allowed some runoff to seep into homes.

Another arctic front swept through during the afternoon of the 14th.  Temperatures dropped from the upper 20s and 30s into the teens in a few hours.  In the wake of the front, wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph were common well into the night.  Some power outages occurred. ||A burst of snow occurred along the front.  Accumulations were mostly 2 inches or less.  However, the snow showers were a bit more persistent across the central mountain counties on south into the Wyoming and McDowell Counties of the southern coal fields.  Here accumulations of 3 to 4 inches were common.  A few spots in the central mountains, around Richwood and Snowshoe had 6 inches of snow accumulation in 12 to 18 hours.||By dawn on the 15th, temperatures dropped into the single digits in the lowlands with a few readings just below zero in the north central counties.  In the mountain counties, the coldest temperature around dawn on the 15th was 15 below zero at Snowshoe.  Bartow observed minus 7.     ||Early on the 15th, wind chill readings of minus 10 to minus 15 were common, but dropped into the minus 20 to minus 30 range over the mountain counties. Despite sunshine on the 15th, temperatures over the central mountains remained on either side of zero degrees, based on elevation.  The clear sky and light winds during the evening of the 15th allowed temperatures to drop below zero in many valley locations over some northern counties.  Before clouds started to increase during the predawn of the 16th, Elkins dropped to 12 below zero, Frost was minus 5, Clarksburg airport was minus 4, Rock Cave was minus 3 and West Union was 2 below zero.

After the arctic deep freeze at dawn on the 20th, snow, sleet, and freezing rain spread over West Virginia between 0300E and 0600E on the 21st. Luckily, it was a Saturday, and travel was limited.  ||A widespread variation of snow totals resulted.  A southeast wind near the ground kept the eastern slopes colder.  This resulted in mostly heavy wet snow, before diminishing and ending as some drizzle during the evening hours.  Snow accumulations of 10 to 20 inches occurred.  For example, Beckley had a 20 inch snowfall in about 15 hours.  The liquid equivalent was 2.10 inches. Snowshoe had a 24 inches snowfall. Their snow depth at Snowshoe went from13 inches prior to the storm, to 35 inches.  At Buckeye on the Greenbrier River, the snow depth went from 8 inches prior to the storm, to 26 inches.  Meanwhile, in northern Pocahontas County at Bartow, their snow depth increased from 7 inches before the storm, to 22 inches.  Roads became impassible between Valley Head and Snowshoe during the afternoon hours.||In the Oak Hill, Mount Hope, and Fayetteville region, accumulations of 10 to 14 inches were seen.  In the Calvin and Craigsville area of Nicholas County 14 to 17 inches of snow was reported by spotters.   Accumulations dropped off into the 6 to 8 inch range in the Tygart River Valley around Elkins and Beverly.  Wyoming and McDowell Counties even saw heavy snow with Ocean reporting 10 inches.  A spotter in McGraws reported 15 inches of snow.  The department of highways storage shed for salt at its Raleigh County headquarters had its roof collapse due to the wet of the heavy snow.  A service station canopy in Beaver also collapsed.  A carport in Simon in Wyoming County collapsed and damaged the parked car.  Snow rolled off of Bolt Mountain and blocked Route 99 to a depth of 15 to 20 feet.         ||Along the Ohio River from Point Pleasant on up the river, the precipitation also stayed mostly as wet snow, but not as heavy.  This included Ravenswood, Parkersburg, Pennsboro, St Marys, and Middlebourne.  In this vicinity, snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were common.  A few 8 inch accumulations were received from southern Wood County and in Ritchie County. ||Meanwhile, between these 2 distinct regions, a milder wedge of air drove northeast during the mid and late morning hours, from southwestern lowlands.  This included areas near Williamson, Logan, and Huntington, on northeast through Charleston.  This milder air quickly reached Glenville, Sutton, Weston, and Grafton.  Snow accumulations here were mostly less than 2 inches, as the initial snow changed to freezing rain.  The freezing rain lasted 3 to 4 hours during the morning. The cold ground temperatures allowed freezing rain to continue even with air temperatures of 33 and 34 degrees.  The ice accumulated to a quarter of an inch. Snow rolled off of Bolt Mountain and blocked Route 99 to a depth of 15 to 20 feet.         ||Along the Ohio River from Point Pleasant on up the river, the precipitation also stayed mostly as wet snow, but not as heavy.  This included Ravenswood, Parkersburg, Pennsboro, St Marys, and Middlebourne.  In this vicinity, snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were common.  A few 8 inch accumulations were received from southern Wood County and in Ritchie County. ||Meanwhile, between these 2 distinct regions, a milder wedge of air drove northeast during the mid and late morning hours, from southwestern lowlands.  This included areas near Williamson, Logan, and Huntington, on northeast through Charleston.  This milder air quickly reached Glenville, Sutton, Weston, and Grafton.  Snow accumulations here were mostly less than 2 inches, as the initial snow changed to freezing rain.  The freezing rain lasted 3 to 4 hours during the morning. The cold ground temperatures allowed freezing rain to continue even with air temperatures of 33 and 34 degrees.  The ice accumulated to a quarter of an inch.  Vehicles were encased in a layer of ice.  Over 2,000 customers lost electricity in Kanawha County, around 1,000 in Wayne and nearly 700 in Cabell County. The freezing rain became mostly rain by midday, with some melting occurring during the afternoon.  ||The rain was heaviest over the southern coal field counties, around Logan and Mingo Counties. The liquid total was over 2 inches.  Melting slush and snow piles from plowing and shoveling prevented the normal drainage of water. Water pooled on many roads.  Ice filled streams were swollen.  Minor flooding occurred around Champmanville, Big Creek, and Shively during the evening.  Water surrounded some mobile homes and blocked low spots on roads.  No significant damage was reported.  Chunks of ice were left when the water receded. Larger streams, such as Twelvepole Creek in Wayne County ran high into Sunday the 22nd.  Ice dams in residential gutters and downspouts allowed some runoff to seep into homes.

After the arctic deep freeze at dawn on the 20th, snow, sleet, and freezing rain spread over West Virginia between 0300E and 0600E on the 21st. Luckily, it was a Saturday, and travel was limited.  ||A widespread variation of snow totals resulted.  A southeast wind near the ground kept the eastern slopes colder.  This resulted in mostly heavy wet snow, before diminishing and ending as some drizzle during the evening hours.  Snow accumulations of 10 to 20 inches occurred.  For example, Beckley had a 20 inch snowfall in about 15 hours.  The liquid equivalent was 2.10 inches. Snowshoe had a 24 inches snowfall. Their snow depth at Snowshoe went from13 inches prior to the storm, to 35 inches.  At Buckeye on the Greenbrier River, the snow depth went from 8 inches prior to the storm, to 26 inches.  Meanwhile, in northern Pocahontas County at Bartow, their snow depth increased from 7 inches before the storm, to 22 inches.  Roads became impassible between Valley Head and Snowshoe during the afternoon hours.||In the Oak Hill, Mount Hope, and Fayetteville region, accumulations of 10 to 14 inches were seen.  In the Calvin and Craigsville area of Nicholas County 14 to 17 inches of snow was reported by spotters.   Accumulations dropped off into the 6 to 8 inch range in the Tygart River Valley around Elkins and Beverly.  Wyoming and McDowell Counties even saw heavy snow with Ocean reporting 10 inches. Another arctic front swept through during the afternoon of the 14th.  Temperatures dropped from the upper 20s and 30s into the teens in a few hours.  In the wake of the front, wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph were common well into the night.  Some power outages occurred. ||A burst of snow occurred along the front.  Accumulations were mostly 2 inches or less.  However, the snow showers were a bit more persistent across the central mountain counties on south into the Wyoming and McDowell Counties of the southern coal fields.  Here accumulations of 3 to 4 inches were common.  A few spots in the central mountains, around Richwood and Snowshoe had 6 inches of snow accumulation in 12 to 18 hours.||By dawn on the 15th, temperatures dropped into the single digits in the lowlands with a few readings just below zero in the north central counties.  In the mountain counties, the coldest temperature around dawn on the 15th was 15 below zero at Snowshoe.  Bartow observed minus 7.     ||Early on the 15th, wind chill readings of minus 10 to minus 15 were common, but dropped into the minus 20 to minus 30 range over the mountain counties. Despite sunshine on the 15th, temperatures over the central mountains remained on either side of zero degrees, based on elevation.  The clear sky and light winds during the evening of the 15th allowed temperatures to drop below zero in many valley locations over some northern counties.  Before clouds started to increase during the predawn of the 16th, Elkins dropped to 12 below zero, Frost was minus 5, Clarksburg airport was minus 4, Rock Cave was minus 3 and West Union was 2 below zero.

A low pressure system moved out of the northeastern Gulf of Mexico and across Georgia during the evening of the 23rd, before moving off the coast on the 24th.||Rains of around three quarters of an inch fell over Raleigh County, before ending as some wet snow during the morning of the 24th.  During the evening of the 23rd, temperatures were hovering on Flat Top Mountain, around Cool Ridge and Ghent.  Ice accumulated on some power lines and elevated metal objects in this area. Very little ice was noted on the warmer ground surfaces. ||The combination of the rain, pockets of freezing rain, a southeast wind gusting near 20 mph across high terrain, and finally a coating of wet snow, caused around 1400 customers to lose electricity in Raleigh County by the morning of the 24th.

A fast moving clipper system moved through on the 6th.  It was aided by a wind speed maximum in the flow aloft.  Surface temperatures were only in the upper teens to lower 20s.||Four to 6 inches of dry snow accumulated in 12 to 15 hours over a portion of the lowland counties in West Virginia.  This was mainly along the Route 50 corridor.  In the more mountainous counties of Barbour, Upshur, and Randolph, snow accumulations of 6 to 8 inches were common.  The maximum snow accumulation reported was near 10 inches from Alpena.  The water content of the snow was mostly 0.25 to 0.40 inches.||This was the first accumulating snow storm of the winter for the northern lowland counties. Further south in West Virginia, the snow amounts were much less, diminishing to zero in the Huntington to Charleston corridor.||The higher snow criteria in north central West Virginia for heavy snow, compared to the mid Ohio Valley, was the determining factor for 2 different event categories in this episode, despite rather similar accumulations.

Steady rain began during the morning on the 10th.  The rain was associated with a warm front.  The rain increased during the late afternoon and evening hours over north central counties.  Rainfall totals were beginning to exceed an inch.  Several small streams were still running well above normal before the rain even began.||Minor flooding along small streams occurred in the usual flood prone areas along roads during the morning hours on the 11th.  The final rainfall total by early morning was 1 to 1.5 inches in north central counties into the central mountains.||A strong rise occurred on headwater rivers overnight into the 11th.  With milder temperatures and more snow melt east of Cheat Mountain compared to the previous week, the upper Greenbrier River caused minor flooding around Marlinton on the 11th.|Other minor river flooding occurred along the Tygart Valley River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along the county border the Middle Fork River caused minor flooding. Boy Scout Camp Road was flooded.  ||A strong rise on the Buckhannon River caused minor overflow, but levels remained just below flood stage.

Snow rolled off of Bolt Mountain and blocked Route 99 to a depth of 15 to 20 feet.         ||Along the Ohio River from Point Pleasant on up the river, the precipitation also stayed mostly as wet snow, but not as heavy.  This included Ravenswood, Parkersburg, Pennsboro, St Marys, and Middlebourne.  In this vicinity, snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were common.  A few 8 inch accumulations were received from southern Wood County and in Ritchie County. ||Meanwhile, between these 2 distinct regions, a milder wedge of air drove northeast during the mid and late morning hours, from southwestern lowlands.  This included areas near Williamson, Logan, and Huntington, on northeast through Charleston.  This milder air quickly reached Glenville, Sutton, Weston, and Grafton.  Snow accumulations here were mostly less than 2 inches, as the initial snow changed to freezing rain.  The freezing rain lasted 3 to 4 hours during the morning. The cold ground temperatures allowed freezing rain to continue even with air temperatures of 33 and 34 degrees.  The ice accumulated to a quarter of an inch.  Vehicles were encased in a layer of ice.  Over 2,000 customers lost electricity in Kanawha County, around 1,000 in Wayne and nearly 700 in Cabell County. The freezing rain became mostly rain by midday, with some melting occurring during the afternoon.  ||The rain was heaviest over the southern coal field counties, around Logan and Mingo Counties. The liquid total was over 2 inches.  Melting slush and snow piles from plowing and shoveling prevented the normal drainage of water. Water pooled on many roads.  Ice filled streams were swollen.  Minor flooding occurred around Champmanville, Big Creek, and Shively during the evening.  Water surrounded some mobile homes and blocked low spots on roads.  No significant damage was reported.  Chunks of ice were left when the water receded. Larger streams, such as Twelvepole Creek in Wayne County ran high into Sunday the 22nd.  Ice dams in residential gutters and downspouts allowed some runoff to seep into homes.

After the arctic deep freeze at dawn on the 20th, snow, sleet, and freezing rain spread over West Virginia between 0300E and 0600E on the 21st. Luckily, it was a Saturday, and travel was limited.  ||A widespread variation of snow totals resulted.  A southeast wind near the ground kept the eastern slopes colder.  This resulted in mostly heavy wet snow, before diminishing and ending as some drizzle during the evening hours.  Snow accumulations of 10 to 20 inches occurred.  For example, Beckley had a 20 inch snowfall in about 15 hours.  The liquid equivalent was 2.10 inches. Snowshoe had a 24 inches snowfall. Their snow depth at Snowshoe went from13 inches prior to the storm, to 35 inches.  At Buckeye on the Greenbrier River, the snow depth went from 8 inches prior to the storm, to 26 inches. Snow showers fell on either side of an arctic cold front.  Snow accumulations of 3 to 6 inches were common during the day and into the evening of the 12th across Randolph, Webster, and Pocahontas Counties.  Lesser amounts fell elsewhere.  Winds increased into the 15 to 25 mph range during the afternoon and evening, as the snow showers diminished to flurries.  Wind chill readings dropped into the minus 10 to minus 15 degree range during the overnight periods across these central mountain counties.  With clearing before dawn on the 13th, the actual air temperatures fell mostly into the zero to 5 below zero range.  However, Elkins did reach 6 below zero.  On the mountaintop at Snowshoe the temperature fell to 10 below zero.  ||Some schools were delayed or canceled.

Wind chill readings dropped into the minus 10 to minus 15 degree range during the overnight periods across these central mountain counties.  With clearing before dawn on the 13th, the actual air temperatures fell mostly into the zero to 5 below zero range.  However, Elkins did reach 6 below zero.  On the mountaintop at Snowshoe the temperature fell to 10 below zero.  ||Some schools were delayed or canceled.

Snow showers fell on either side of an arctic cold front.  Snow accumulations of 3 to 6 inches were common during the day and into the evening of the 12th across Randolph, Webster, and Pocahontas Counties.  Lesser amounts fell elsewhere.  Winds increased into the 15 to 25 mph range during the afternoon and evening, as the snow showers diminished to flurries.  Wind chill readings dropped into the minus 10 to minus 15 degree range during the overnight periods across these central mountain counties.  With clearing before dawn on the 13th, the actual air temperatures fell mostly into the zero to 5 below zero range.  However, Elkins did reach 6 below zero.  On the mountaintop at Snowshoe the temperature fell to 10 below zero.  ||Some schools were delayed or canceled.

Another arctic front swept through during the afternoon of the 14th.  Temperatures dropped from the upper 20s and 30s into the teens in a few hours.  In the wake of the front, wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph were common well into the night.  Some power outages occurred. ||A burst of snow occurred along the front.  Accumulations were mostly 2 inches or less.  However, the snow showers were a bit more persistent across the central mountain counties on south into the Wyoming and McDowell Counties of the southern coal fields.  Here accumulations of 3 to 4 inches were common.  A few spots in the central mountains, around Richwood and Snowshoe had 6 inches of snow accumulation in 12 to 18 hours.||By dawn on the 15th, temperatures dropped into the single digits in the lowlands with a few readings just below zero in the north central counties.  In the mountain counties, the coldest temperature around dawn on the 15th was 15 below zero at Snowshoe.  Bartow observed minus 7.     ||Early on the 15th, wind chill readings of minus 10 to minus 15 were common, but dropped into the minus 20 to minus 30 range over the mountain counties. Despite sunshine on the 15th, temperatures over the central mountains remained on either side of zero degrees, based on elevation.  The clear sky and light winds during the evening of the 15th allowed temperatures to drop below zero in many valley locations over some northern counties.  Before clouds started to increase during the predawn of the 16th, Elkins dropped to 12 below zero, Frost was minus 5, Clarksburg airport was minus 4, Rock Cave was minus 3 and West Union was 2 below zero.

A fast moving clipper system moved through on the 6th.  It was aided by a wind speed maximum in the flow aloft.  Surface temperatures were only in the upper teens to lower 20s.||Four to 6 inches of dry snow accumulated in 12 to 15 hours over a portion of the lowland counties in West Virginia.  This was mainly along the Route 50 corridor.  In the more mountainous counties of Barbour, Upshur, and Randolph, snow accumulations of 6 to 8 inches were common.  The maximum snow accumulation reported was near 10 inches from Alpena.  The water content of the snow was mostly 0.25 to 0.40 inches.||This was the first accumulating snow storm of the winter for the northern lowland counties. Further south in West Virginia, the snow amounts were much less, diminishing to zero in the Huntington to Charleston corridor.||The higher snow criteria in north central West Virginia for heavy snow, compared to the mid Ohio Valley, was the determining factor for 2 different event categories in this episode, despite rather similar accumulations.

Another very strong Arctic cold front plunged through the forecast area allowing temperatures to plummet to record cold levels and definitely the coldest since January 2014.  Strong low-level cold advection and northwest upslope flow supported a period of upslope snow showers across the western mountains, some of which spread further east toward the Blue Ridge.  In addition, a pre-frontal upper-level impulse also provided for some light snow across the Piedmont.  The snowfall combined with quick freezing of any water on the roadways caused a number of traffic issues along Interstate 77 in Mercer county. ||Here are the snowfall amounts observed in the West Virginia counties within the National Weather Service Blacksburg, Virginia forecast area:||Western Greenbrier County - 4.0 inches at Quinwood to 5.0 inches at McRoss,|Eastern Greenbrier County - 1.0 inch at White Sulphur Springs to 3.0 inches at Lewisburg,|Mercer County - 3.0 inches at Princeton,|Summers County - 1.0 inch 2SSE Hinton to 4.0 inches at Jumping Branch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Summers County 911 Center reported 4.0 inches of snow at Jumping Branch, while the Hinton COOP observer measured only 1.0 inch of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused Deckers Creek to go above flood stage of 3.0 feet overnight on the 10th through the evening of the 11th. The subterranean bridge and roadway adjacent to the creek were flooded.

A large land slide occurred on the 12th at the end of the main runway at Yeager Airport.  The slide was part of a large man-made land fill project that extended the hill side. That project was completed in 2007. The vertical rise of that land fill was about 240 feet.  The extension was for a safety system at the end of a runway at Yeager Airport. ||The huge land slide reached the hollow below.  It engulfed Keystone Drive, 2 houses and a church.  Eventually, the slide blocked the creek called Elk Two Mile.  The creek was swollen from recent rain and snow melt, but was not flooding prior to the slide.  The water backed up behind the slide and flooded 9 homes along Keystone Drive during the day on the 13th.  Many of those home had already been evacuated because of the land slide.  The water reached its peak before 1000E on the 13th.  Contractors dug a diversion channel around the slide to allow Elk Two Mile to drain.  ||Meanwhile, a warm front brought more rain back to West Virginia during the late afternoon and evening on the 13th.  The additional rain did not cause any more major problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man-made extension to a steep hill side was constructed and finished back in 2007.  It was located at the end of the main runway at Yeager Airport.  The large land fill allowed space for an engineered material arresting system for airplane safety. ||A huge section of that steep hill side came sliding down just after 11000E on the 12th.  Through the night and into the early morning hours of the 13th, the slide continued to fan out in the hollow below.  A church and 2 houses were destroyed by the slide.  Power lines and poles were also destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near calm winds, a clear sky, and a snow cover allowed early morning temperatures to drop below zero across northern West Virginia.||The coldest official temperature was 21 degrees below zero at Glady in Randolph County.  The airport at Elkins dropped to a record low for the date with minus 15.  In Pocahontas County, Bartow observed minus 12 while Marlington was 9 below zero.  Middlebourne of Tyler County was also minus 9.   In northern Jackson County near Sandyville, a minus 10 was observed.  West Union had 5 below zero. ||Parkersburg dropped to minus 8, also a record for the date.  Downtown Clarksburg and Grafton were at 4 below zero.  Grantsville and Glenville were both at 3 below zero.||Public schools were canceled or on a 2 hour delayed start for the day.
