EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southerly flow resulted in very warm moist air entering the region leading up to the early morning hours of March 5th. Precipitable water values were observed ranging from one inch west of interstate 77 to around 1.25 inches east of the Blue Ridge. During the evening of March 4th, a Miller A low pressure system passed across the southeast states, radiating moisture northward along the Appalachian chain, with areas of heavy rain observed across southeast West Virginia. Arctic air built in from the west during the early morning hours of March 5th, causing rain to change over to areas of freezing rain, sleet and snow. Highest elevations along the interstate 64 changed over to a wintry mix before dawn, with the changeover gradually working into the lower elevations across the mountains around sunrise. A wave of low pressure passed across the Piedmont during the late morning of the 5th, enhancing wintry precipitation across the mountains. As the wave passed north during the afternoon, precipitation came to an end from west to east as winds shifted northwesterly, allowing drier arctic air to move into the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported five inches of snowfall across the western half of Greenbrier County, in addition one and a half inches of sleet and a few hundredths of an inch of freezing rain.

These showers caused flooding overnight into the 9th across northern counties.  Most of the flooding was minor.||Distinct thunderstorm cells formed during the afternoon on the 9th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams rose quickly and blocked roads including Route 74 and Lamberton Road.  A rain gauge near Ellenboro measured 1.8 inches in 24 hours, most of which fell in less than 6 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of dry weather allowed rivers and streams to recede before slow-moving low pressure over the Ohio valley lifted north within a broad upper level trough that was pushing into a building subtropical ridge off the southeast U.S. coast. 24-hour rainfall ending 12Z on the 11th was heaviest over the western mountains with amounts ranging from 0.50 to 1 inch in southeast West Virginia.  A number of areal Flood Warnings were issued as snowmelt made a considerable contribution to the runoff. The most significant flooding occurred along the Meadow River in Greenbrier County where the river flooded several roads and approached a number of structures in the floodplain. The newly installed IFLOWS gage at Hines (HNEW2) reached 14.9 feet which was above the Minor flood stage of 14 feet for that reach of the river. Parts of Route 60 east of Rainelle were closed by flooding from the river. The extensive snowmelt and rainfall contributed to a landslide near Ronceverte in Greenbrier County that contributed to a fatality. A 68-year old woman was killed when her car was struck by a tree amidst the debris of the landslide on the afternoon of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was considerable flooding along the Meadow River from Hines to Rainelle. The stream gage on the Meadow River at Hines (HNEW2) crested at 14.9 feet (Minor Flood Stage = 14 feet). Part of Route 60 was closed near Charmco. Buggs and Sewell Creeks in Rainelle were also flooded.

The snow reached the Little Kanawha Valley and the central mountains during the late morning hours. The temperature was only 5 to 10 degrees at the start of the storm.  The snow increased during the late morning into the afternoon, then decreased that evening.  The snow ended early on the 17th.||All during the storm, the temperature hovered on either side of 10 degrees.   Snow accumulations of 7 to 11 inches were common from the Huntington, Charleston to Marlinton corridor, on south into the southern coal field counties.  Further north, accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were measured.  For example, Pineville had 12 inches of snow, while Logan measured an 11 inch accumulation. Summersville, Marlinton and the city of Beckley had 10 inches.  Huntington had a 9 inch accumulation.  Charleston had 7 inches.  Sutton had a 6 inch accumulation.  The maximum accumulations from unofficial sources were near 14 inches from Mallory of Logan County and Oceana of Wyoming County.  Snow amounts were less toward Middlebourne, West Union, and Clarksburg.    ||This was the first significant snow storm of the 2014-15 winter for southern counties.

A strong southerly flow resulted in very warm moist air entering the region leading up to the early morning hours of March 5th. Precipitable water values were observed ranging from one inch west of interstate 77 to around 1.25 inches east of the Blue Ridge. During the evening of March 4th, a Miller A low pressure system passed across the southeast states, radiating moisture northward along the Appalachian chain, with areas of heavy rain observed across southeast West Virginia. Arctic air built in from the west during the early morning hours of March 5th, causing rain to change over to areas of freezing rain, sleet and snow. Highest elevations along the interstate 64 changed over to a wintry mix before dawn, with the changeover gradually working into the lower elevations across the mountains around sunrise. A wave of low pressure passed across the Piedmont during the late morning of the 5th, enhancing wintry precipitation across the mountains. As the wave passed north during the afternoon, precipitation came to an end from west to east as winds shifted northwesterly, allowing drier arctic air to move into the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 6 inches across Monroe County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms existed at dawn on the 8th in Indiana.  With support for the mid and upper level winds, this mesoscale convective complex maintained its intensity through the morning.  It raced southeast at 50 mph into western West Virginia during the afternoon. Most areas saw brief wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph and rains of a quarter to a half inch.  A stronger wind gust was observed by the Elkins airport.||A few stronger cells formed on the southwestern inflow portion of this complex and moved into Mingo and McDowell Counties.||After a lull, additional rounds of showers and thunderstorms moved into West Virginia after sunset.  These showers caused flooding overnight into the 9th across northern counties.  Most of the flooding was minor.||Distinct thunderstorm cells formed during the afternoon on the 9th.  Hail was associated with the stronger storms.  One storm, that showed considerable mid level rotation moved into Wood County.  No evidence of a tornado was found. ||Widespread showers along the cold front followed during the morning of the 10th. This final round of showers caused some minor flooding.  Middlebourne had 1.8 inches of rain from both the Thursday convection and the final round of showers early on the 10th.||In the wake of the cold front, drier air finally moved into the state during the afternoon of the 10th.||Eventually, another disaster was declared by the federal government.  See FEMA disaster 4220 for more information.

Widespread precipitation formed as energy transferred from low pressure moving through the Ohio River Valley to a developing coastal low off the Carolinas. Snow was brief as a strengthening low level jet aided in transitioning the precipitation to rain by midday, limiting snow totals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow between 3.0 and 5.0 inches was reported.

Brief showers and thunderstorms formed during the mid and late afternoon ahead of a cold front.  Due to the low freezing level, small hail occurred with the strongest cells.  One trained spotter measured hail to three quarters of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail as large as three quarters of an inch briefly fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought widespread heavy snow to the area with a strong convergence zone aligning across northern Virginia into eastern Maryland resulting in mesoscale banding and higher snow totals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow between 7.0 and 10.0 inches was reported around the county and in surrounding areas.

A warm front was draped across the Mason-Dixon Line as a cold front slowly approached from the northwest, leaving the Mid Atlantic warm sectored. Increasing instability and shear ahead of the approaching front coupled with a passing jet streak providing forcing aloft led to scattered strong to severe thunderstorms.

A wet period began early on the 4th as a complex series of disturbances lifted northeast through the Ohio Valley pushing a slow-moving cold front across the area. Rainfall ending 0700 EST on March 4th was confined to the northwestern mountains with amounts to that time from 0.25 to 0.75 inches. Stream gages across parts of the western New and Greenbrier  basins were rising rapidly during the overnight and early morning hours of March 4th. The wet soils and rapid snowmelt were strong contributors to the rapid runoff. Snow water equivalents per the National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center (NOHRSC) in parts of these river basins ranged from around 1��� to as much as 4��� in the higher terrain at the outset of the flooding. The rain and warmer temperatures caused rapid snowmelt the entire day on the 4th along with continued rainfall and there were numerous reports of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed by flooding in the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow between 7.0 and 11.0 inches was reported around the county and in surrounding areas.

A wet period began early on the 4th as a complex series of disturbances lifted northeast through the Ohio Valley pushing a slow-moving cold front across the area. Rainfall ending 0700 EST on March 4th was confined to the northwestern mountains with amounts to that time from 0.25 to 0.75 inches. Stream gages across parts of the western New and Greenbrier  basins were rising rapidly during the overnight and early morning hours of March 4th. The wet soils and rapid snowmelt were strong contributors to the rapid runoff. Snow water equivalents per the National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center (NOHRSC) in parts of these river basins ranged from around 1��� to as much as 4��� in the higher terrain at the outset of the flooding. The rain and warmer temperatures caused rapid snowmelt the entire day on the 4th along with continued rainfall and there were numerous reports of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding was reported across the county. Around two dozen roads were reported closed by WV DOT and significant road damage occurred also per WV DOT. The Bluestone River in Mercer County flooded through much of the county with a peak crest above the Moderate Flood Stage (17 feet) at the Spanishburg gage (SPNW2), reaching 17.1 feet early on the 5th. Route 19 was closed in six places along a 3-mile stretch by flooding from the Bluestone River according to the county Emergency Services Director, but no evacuations were required.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought widespread heavy snow to the area with a strong convergence zone aligning across northern Virginia into eastern Maryland resulting in mesoscale banding and higher snow totals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow between 7.0 and 10.0 inches was reported around the county and in surrounding areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms existed at dawn on the 8th in Indiana.  With support for the mid and upper level winds, this mesoscale convective complex maintained its intensity through the morning.  It raced southeast at 50 mph into western West Virginia during the afternoon. Most areas saw brief wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph and rains of a quarter to a half inch.  A stronger wind gust was observed by the Elkins airport.||A few stronger cells formed on the southwestern inflow portion of this complex and moved into Mingo and McDowell Counties.||After a lull, additional rounds of showers and thunderstorms moved into West Virginia after sunset.  These showers caused flooding overnight into the 9th across northern counties.  Most of the flooding was minor.||Distinct thunderstorm cells formed during the afternoon on the 9th.  Hail was associated with the stronger storms.  One storm, that showed considerable mid level rotation moved into Wood County.  No evidence of a tornado was found. ||Widespread showers along the cold front followed during the morning of the 10th. This final round of showers caused some minor flooding.  Middlebourne had 1.8 inches of rain from both the Thursday convection and the final round of showers early on the 10th.||In the wake of the cold front, drier air finally moved into the state during the afternoon of the 10th.||Eventually, another disaster was declared by the federal government.  See FEMA disaster 4220 for more information.

A warm front was draped across the Mason-Dixon Line as a cold front slowly approached from the northwest, leaving the Mid Atlantic warm sectored. Increasing instability and shear ahead of the approaching front coupled with a passing jet streak providing forcing aloft led to scattered strong to severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down in the road near the 6000 block of River Rd.

A fast moving area of low pressure moving along a cold front produced a quick 1 to 3 inches of snow the morning of the 22nd. The best accumulation was in the higher elevations of western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer reported 2 inches of snow quickly fell during the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure wedging combining with clearing skies resulted in temperatures dropping below freezing. An upper level disturbance passing to the north brought light precipitation to the area. Warm air advection aloft led to the precipitation falling mainly as freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around a trace of ice was reported in surrounding areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure lifting from the Ohio River Valley into the eastern Great Lakes dragged a cold front through the region. Southerly flow ahead of the front resulted in high moisture advection and with temperatures hovering in the 20s, moderate to heavy snow was reported across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter in Kline Gap reported snow total of 7.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The system that brought mixed precipitation and a gradual changeover to rain on the 3rd continued to plague the region on the 4th as the cold front was slowly pushed across the Upper Ohio Valley by an approaching upper level trough. This brought a transition from rain to snow across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland through the evening hours of the 4th, into the 5th. Six to twelve inches of heavy snow was reported in several counties in OH, WV, MD and southern PA through early afternoon on the 5th.
A strong cold front moving through brought a quick moderate snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 2.0 and 4.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwest flow ushered in plenty of moisture around high pressure over the Southeast Coast. The heat combined with the moisture to cause moderate amounts of instability. A few thunderstorms developed, and some became severe due to the unstable atmosphere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were down along Route 9 near Wiltshire Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total ice around 0.10 inches was reported near Martinsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ridging building in aloft as high pressure remainded at the surface led to clearing skies and light winds. Lingering moisture combining with the synoptic features produce areas of valley fog.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow between 3.0 and 5.0 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two distinct convection lines formed during the afternoon and moved southeast during the early evening.  There were still gaps between the cells in both lines. One line was well ahead of a surface cold front.  The other line was just ahead of the wind shift associated with a surface cold front.

A cold front moving southeast from the eastern Great Lakes through the Upper Ohio Valley produced widespread showers and thunderstorms. Some of the thunderstorms were severe with damaging winds most commonly reported. The thunderstorms also produced torrential rainfall at times, with flash flooding reported across portions of eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 call center reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed in central Ohio in response to a disturbance in the winds aloft. As the thunderstorms moved southeast, a diffuse line formed and moved into western West Virginia. Other storms formed out ahead of the main line in southern counties.  Most areas saw gusty winds of 30 to 50 mph ahead of the main convection.
Ample moisture ahead of a backdoor cold front resulted in dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MRB reported quarter mile visibility for multiple hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sporadic strong winds in the 40 to 45 mph range late Tuesday, October, 28 and early Wednesday, October, 29 brought down some trees in the Bluefield, Bluewell, and Bluestone Valley regions of Mercer County, WV. Trees were reported down on Oakhurst Avenue, Louden Street, and Route 460 in Bluefield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sporadic strong winds in the 40 to 45 mph range late Tuesday, October, 28 and early Wednesday, October, 29 brought down some trees in the Bluefield, Bluewell, and Bluestone Valley regions of Mercer County, WV. Trees were reported down on Oakhurst Avenue, Louden Street, and Route 460 in Bluefield. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow line of showers, associated with a secondary front, caused gusty predawn winds on the 29th.  Most wind gusts were similar to the low level wind flow, in the 30 to 40 mph range. However, a gust to 50 mph was recorded at Yeager Airport.||A tree fell onto power lines along Autumn Drive in Dunbar.

A wet period began early on the 4th as a complex series of disturbances lifted northeast through the Ohio Valley pushing a slow-moving cold front across the area. Rainfall ending 0700 EST on March 4th was confined to the northwestern mountains with amounts to that time from 0.25 to 0.75 inches. Stream gages across parts of the western New and Greenbrier  basins were rising rapidly during the overnight and early morning hours of March 4th. The wet soils and rapid snowmelt were strong contributors to the rapid runoff. Snow water equivalents per the National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center (NOHRSC) in parts of these river basins ranged from around 1��� to as much as 4��� in the higher terrain at the outset of the flooding. The rain and warmer temperatures caused rapid snowmelt the entire day on the 4th along with continued rainfall and there were numerous reports of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of flooding across the county, mainly roadways. A road near Beech Knob was reportedly washed out and a number of roads were closed by flooding. Tuckahoe Road was closed at  Tuckahoe Tunnel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture advection ahead of an approaching cold front aided in the development of fog over the Potomac Highlands, with patchy dense fog across Eastern Grant county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: W99 reported reduced visibilities of one quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure wave rode east through Kentucky during the late afternoon and evening, along a strong east to west front.  There was a 30 degree temperature contrast on either side of the front, say from around Huntington to southeast Kentucky.||A few discrete thunderstorms cells formed ahead of the main cluster of convection.  These cells moved through extreme southern West Virginia during the evening hours.

Lines of showers and thunderstorms streaked through West Virginia ahead of a large board mid level trough in the upper Midwest. This convection was also hundreds of miles ahead of the surface cold front.  The first line moved through during the early and mid afternoon, while another line of storms moved through during the early evening.  Their movement was toward the northeast at 40 to 45 mph. Gusty winds and downpours were common.  The rain was beneficial for many areas. However, a few storms pulsed briefly to stronger levels during the afternoon storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple cellular convection formed during the early afternoon across eastern Kentucky and southern West Virginia.  A few storms pulsed to stronger levels.||The most public impact appeared to be from a storm that quickly collapsed in the Saint Albans, Nitro, and Cross Lanes vicinity of Kanawha County.  Fallen trees caused around 8,000 customers to be without electricity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees on Poplar Fork Road, near the Kanawha County border, were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shift to northwest flow aloft on the 12th initiated a series of thunderstorm complexes to drop southeast across the area. Several pockets of heavy rainfall occurred across parts of the during the overnight and early morning hours of July 12-13.  Rainfall of 1 to 3 inches in a few hours caused isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anjean Road was flooded out about 2 miles from Rupert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a northwest flow aloft, clusters of showers and thunderstorms formed during the evening hours on the 12th.  Storms were along a weak front, and ahead of a weak low pressure system in southern Ohio. The clusters of showers and thunderstorms became aligned northwest to southwest along the flow.   Two distinct areas of heavy rain occurred.  One was over eastern Wood County around Mineralwells during the evening of the 12th.  The other area was toward dawn on the 13th over the headwaters of the Elk River from Sutton to Webster Springs and to a lesser extent south into Nicholas County around Richwood.   The heaviest measured rain was 3.37 inches from Sutton.  Webster Springs observed 3.06 inches, and Criagsville 2.45 inches.  Estimates of at least that order of magnitude likely fell around Mineralwells. ||After a lull in the rain during the mid morning into the mid afternoon a mesoscale convective complex dropped southeast, through central and southern West Virginia during the late afternoon and early evening.  This caused mostly minor wind damage from fallen trees. ||Another lull followed during the late evening.  However, in the humid air, more thunderstorms formed in southeast Ohio and moved into southern West Virginia after midnight, for the early morning hours on the 14th.  The axis of heaviest rain went southeast from Mason and Cabell Counties on through Putnam, Lincoln, southwestern Kanawha, and into Boone Counties.  The 24 hour rain totals of 2 to 3.5 inches were measured.   For example, a spotter in Hurricane had 3.3 inches of rain.  The cooperative observer at Madison had 2.65 inches of rain.  The automatic gauge at Hager Cemetery in Lincoln County measured 2.36 inches of rain.  Flash flooding was common.  ||Finally, more thunderstorms formed in northern Ohio ahead of a cold front and mid level disturbance during the midday and early afternoon on the 14th.  These storms formed into a squall line and moved southeast, through West Virginia during the late afternoon and early evening.  Rain amounts of .5 to 1 in an hour were enough to cause a few minor flooding problems again over the waterlogged counties in the south.  Yet, the main effect was strong winds on the leading edge of the squall line.  This caused electrical power outages.||In the aftermath, a gubernatorial state of emergency was declared for a few counties.  Eventually, a federal disaster was declared by President Obama for the period July 10th through the 14th.  It included 8 counties in West Virginia.  See FEMA disaster 4236 for more information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light winds and clear skies as a result of building high pressure led to patchy dense fog development over Eastern Grant county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: W99 reported reduced visibilities of one quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of the 26th, a low pressure system was organizing over the lower Ohio River.  Meanwhile, ahead of that system, a frontal boundary stretched east through southern Ohio and central West Virginia.  ||Thunderstorms formed along the frontal boundary in southern Ohio, with more widespread rain north of the front.  Those thunderstorms moved slightly south of east into western West Virginia. One of the leading storms was severe with mid level rotation.  It pulsed to its strongest level near Ripley of Jackson County. This caused widespread tree damage from Ripley east along Route 33.  One brief EF0 was also declared from a storm survey.  The strongest winds were estimated with the downburst winds rather than the tornado.||Repetitive thunderstorms and showers affected northern Mason County and central Jackson County.  Downpours occurred.  An automatic gauge at Mason in Mason County measured 1.88 in the hour from 1730 to 1830E.  Its total in 3 hours was 2.55 inches.  The Ripley water plant measured 2.23 inches, most of which fell in a hour.||One location along the headwaters of Stationcamp Run along Route 5/13 saw large hail, followed by severe wind gusts. The downpour then caused flash flooding in the same location.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were flooded and closed. Minor road damage occurred near Letart.  Water surrounded one home along Dewhurst Road near Broad Run.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another day of heat, combined with moisture and instability, to produce isolated to scattered thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening hours. Yet, no significant flooding occurred due to the localized nature of the heavy rain and the relatively dry ground.||One storm pulsed briefly to stronger levels in central West Virginia.

Culverts were washed out or damaged, including a large 5 foot drain pipe. ||The water crested 27 inches deep in the Duval Fire Department. The assistant chief of the fire department said, It looked like white water rafting. You wouldn't know it was a river or a road.  An auto repair shop was damaged.  The water was 2 to 3 feet into the Griffithsville Post Office. In Yawkey, 2 ambulances at the medic station got water inside.  Further south, 9 Mile Creek flooded roads near Ranger.||Initial damage assessment had about a dozen homes with major damage and around 50 homes with minor damage.  In the wake of the flood, a few dozen homes were inaccessible due to damaged private bridges or driveways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall developed across portions of the southern Appalachian mountains as clearing ahead of a slow-moving surface low over the Cumberland Gap allowed instabilities to rise into the 2000 J/Kg range along with good upper divergence. Developing storms and cell mergers produced rainfall rates of 1.5���/hr with totals up to 3-4��� in isolated storms. A Flash Flood Warning was issued for Mercer County at 737 PM EDT where radar had showed 1 to 2 inches of rain had fallen with more expected. Total rainfall amounts reached 2.5 to 3 inches in a 3-hour period ending around 02z (10 PM on the 5th) over parts of western Mercer County which produced substantial flash flooding and debris flows in several locations. An IFLOWS rain gage at Bramwell, WV recorded  3.47��� in a 3-hour period. This amount/duration was right around the 200-year annual recurrence interval (ARI) per NOAA Atlas 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow was reported along Route 52 near Bramwell.

Two distinct areas of heavy rain occurred.  One was over eastern Wood County around Mineralwells during the evening of the 12th.  The other area was toward dawn on the 13th over the headwaters of the Elk River from Sutton to Webster Springs and to a lesser extent south into Nicholas County around Richwood.   The heaviest measured rain was 3.37 inches from Sutton.  Webster Springs observed 3.06 inches, and Criagsville 2.45 inches.  Estimates of at least that order of magnitude likely fell around Mineralwells. ||After a lull in the rain during the mid morning into the mid afternoon a mesoscale convective complex dropped southeast, through central and southern West Virginia during the late afternoon and early evening.  This caused mostly minor wind damage from fallen trees. ||Another lull followed during the late evening.  However, in the humid air, more thunderstorms formed in southeast Ohio and moved into southern West Virginia after midnight, for the early morning hours on the 14th.  The axis of heaviest rain went southeast from Mason and Cabell Counties on through Putnam, Lincoln, southwestern Kanawha, and into Boone Counties.  The 24 hour rain totals of 2 to 3.5 inches were measured.   For example, a spotter in Hurricane had 3.3 inches of rain.  The cooperative observer at Madison had 2.65 inches of rain.  The automatic gauge at Hager Cemetery in Lincoln County measured 2.36 inches of rain.  Flash flooding was common.  ||Finally, more thunderstorms formed in northern Ohio ahead of a cold front and mid level disturbance during the midday and early afternoon on the 14th.  These storms formed into a squall line and moved southeast, through West Virginia during the late afternoon and early evening.  Rain amounts of .5 to 1 in an hour were enough to cause a few minor flooding problems again over the waterlogged counties in the south.  Yet, the main effect was strong winds on the leading edge of the squall line.  This caused electrical power outages.||In the aftermath, a gubernatorial state of emergency was declared for a few counties.  Eventually, a federal disaster was declared by President Obama for the period July 10th through the 14th.  It included 8 counties in West Virginia.  See FEMA disaster 4236 for more information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Centralia Road.

The 24 hour rain totals of 2 to 3.5 inches were measured.   For example, a spotter in Hurricane had 3.3 inches of rain.  The cooperative observer at Madison had 2.65 inches of rain.  The automatic gauge at Hager Cemetery in Lincoln County measured 2.36 inches of rain.  Flash flooding was common.  ||Finally, more thunderstorms formed in northern Ohio ahead of a cold front and mid level disturbance during the midday and early afternoon on the 14th.  These storms formed into a squall line and moved southeast, through West Virginia during the late afternoon and early evening.  Rain amounts of .5 to 1 in an hour were enough to cause a few minor flooding problems again over the waterlogged counties in the south.  Yet, the main effect was strong winds on the leading edge of the squall line.  This caused electrical power outages.||In the aftermath, a gubernatorial state of emergency was declared for a few counties.  Eventually, a federal disaster was declared by President Obama for the period July 10th through the 14th.  It included 8 counties in West Virginia.  See FEMA disaster 4236 for more information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a large cluster of showers and thunderstorms, new storms formed over western Kanawha County during the evening hours.  These storms drifted north during the night, producing over 2 inches of rain in less than 3 hours.  Since the ground was initially dry, only minor flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads flooded from around Tornado on north through Saint Albans and Cross Lanes.  A few water rescues occurred in Cross Lanes when vehicles stalled in high water. Water surrounded homes along Route 62 and Armour Creek in Cross Lanes. Water got into basements in both Cross Lanes and Saint Albans. ||A National Weather Service employee along Angel Fork measured a total of 3.16 inches of rain, most of which fell in less than 2 hours.  Another spotter along Browns Creek measured 3 inches.  A nearby automated gauge at Tornado had 2.65 inches.  The cooperative observer in Saint Albans measured 1.9 inches of rain.||During the rain, a tractor-trailer on Interstate 64 hydroplaned on the east bound lanes between Cross Lanes and Institute.  The truck spun 180 degrees, hit a guard rail, and then flipped over onto its top. The driver was taken to the hospital, but was expected to recover.  The truck's diesel spilled onto the interstate.  It took highway crews into the daylight hours of the 18th to reopen those lanes of the interstate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streams quickly flooded.  One example was Scary Creek with 3 feet of water over the road.  There was significant urban flooding with drainage into basements. Buffalo Creek flooded the county fair grounds near Eleanor.||The emergency manager reported about 3 homes in the Poca vicinity with damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly flow and moderate instability ahead of an approaching cold front helped to spawn showers and thunderstorms, some of which were severe, across the northern West Virginia pan-handle, and western Pennsylvania on the 7th. With dry air present aloft, several of the storms produced damaging wind gusts that brought down trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local 911 center reported trees down on 12th Street in Moundsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly flow and moderate instability ahead of an approaching cold front helped to spawn showers and thunderstorms, some of which were severe, across the northern West Virginia pan-handle, and western Pennsylvania on the 7th. With dry air present aloft, several of the storms produced damaging wind gusts that brought down trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local 911 center reported trees down at the intersection of Route 88 and Pine Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lines of showers and thunderstorms developed in advance of an approaching cold front. Some of these storms contained damaging winds that downed trees, and one wind gust snapped a power pole.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed two trees within the community of Crichton and downed several in the community of Quinwood. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a northwest flow aloft, clusters of showers and thunderstorms formed during the evening hours on the 12th.  Storms were along a weak front, and ahead of a weak low pressure system in southern Ohio. The clusters of showers and thunderstorms became aligned northwest to southwest along the flow.   Two distinct areas of heavy rain occurred.  One was over eastern Wood County around Mineralwells during the evening of the 12th.  The other area was toward dawn on the 13th over the headwaters of the Elk River from Sutton to Webster Springs and to a lesser extent south into Nicholas County around Richwood.   The heaviest measured rain was 3.37 inches from Sutton.  Webster Springs observed 3.06 inches, and Criagsville 2.45 inches.  Estimates of at least that order of magnitude likely fell around Mineralwells. ||After a lull in the rain during the mid morning into the mid afternoon a mesoscale convective complex dropped southeast, through central and southern West Virginia during the late afternoon and early evening.  This caused mostly minor wind damage from fallen trees. ||Another lull followed during the late evening.  However, in the humid air, more thunderstorms formed in southeast Ohio and moved into southern West Virginia after midnight, for the early morning hours on the 14th.  The axis of heaviest rain went southeast from Mason and Cabell Counties on through Putnam, Lincoln, southwestern Kanawha, and into Boone Counties.  The 24 hour rain totals of 2 to 3.5 inches were measured.   For example, a spotter in Hurricane had 3.3 inches of rain.  The cooperative observer at Madison had 2.65 inches of rain.  The automatic gauge at Hager Cemetery in Lincoln County measured 2.36 inches of rain.  Flash flooding was common.  ||Finally, more thunderstorms formed in northern Ohio ahead of a cold front and mid level disturbance during the midday and early afternoon on the 14th.  These storms formed into a squall line and moved southeast, through West Virginia during the late afternoon and early evening.  Rain amounts of .5 to 1 in an hour were enough to cause a few minor flooding problems again over the waterlogged counties in the south.  Yet, the main effect was strong winds on the leading edge of the squall line.  This caused electrical power outages.||In the aftermath, a gubernatorial state of emergency was declared for a few counties.  Eventually, a federal disaster was declared by President Obama for the period July 10th through the 14th.  It included 8 counties in West Virginia.  See FEMA disaster 4236 for more information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 65 homes had some degree of flooding around Mineralwells according to state and county emergency officials. The American Red Cross surveyed 24 apartments, 14 mobile homes, and about 24 homes damaged.  The Mount Pleasant Estates subdivision had water reaching some car windows.  Some residents went to their roof tops to ride out the high water.  Water was also up to the windows of an apartment complex.  The waters from Little Tygart Creek completely cut through Lincolnshire Street and washed out a culvert.  A travel trailer at the fair grounds was lifted up by the flood waters and started to float.  Its occupant tried to climb onto its roof, but was unharmed when the trailer ran aground.  A car was submerged along Route 14.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were flooded, damaged, and closed around Sutton, including Route 4 along Granny Creek.  Centralia Road was heavily damaged and undermined in sections by the Left Fork and Spruce Fork. Residents along Centralia Road lost water when a water pipe was exposed. A water rescue also took place from a vehicle stalled in flood waters.  Even the water on downtown streets in Sutton was 6 to 12 inches deep. Culverts were washed out and numerous road closures occurred around Little Birch.  ||Many campgrounds in the headwaters of Sutton Lake were flooded.  Yet, that was by design.  Runoff was stored behind the dam, causing the lake elevation to rise several feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed by flooding streams or runoff and debris off mountainsides.  This included Route 15 and 20 between Webster Springs and Diana.  Runoff came off the steep mountainsides and ran through the streets of Webster Springs.  Water came down off of Spillman Mountain. The mountainside had a 26 percent grade or slope. The small unnamed stream overflowed.  Its water ran down Euclyde Street and torn up the asphalt on Miller Mountain Road in front of the hospital.  Water got inside the hospital too, causing it to close temporarily.  The kitchen area of the hospital was most affected.  A few windows were shattered. ||The emergency room opened shortly after the flooding.  Inpatient services were up and running a few days after the flood through the use of a mobile kitchen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Surface low pressure system, supported by a strong shortwave aloft, moved from the upper Midwest toward Pennsylvania by the afternoon on the 9th. Breaks in the cloud cover ahead of the associated cold front allowed for the region to destabilize, supporting the development of showers and thunderstorms. Some of these storms became severe and a small bowing line developed, which brought down trees across southwestern Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County official reported a tree down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a northwest flow aloft, clusters of showers and thunderstorms formed during the evening hours on the 12th.  Storms were along a weak front, and ahead of a weak low pressure system in southern Ohio. The clusters of showers and thunderstorms became aligned northwest to southwest along the flow.   Two distinct areas of heavy rain occurred.  One was over eastern Wood County around Mineralwells during the evening of the 12th.  The other area was toward dawn on the 13th over the headwaters of the Elk River from Sutton to Webster Springs and to a lesser extent south into Nicholas County around Richwood.   The heaviest measured rain was 3.37 inches from Sutton.  Webster Springs observed 3.06 inches, and Criagsville 2.45 inches.  Estimates of at least that order of magnitude likely fell around Mineralwells. ||After a lull in the rain during the mid morning into the mid afternoon a mesoscale convective complex dropped southeast, through central and southern West Virginia during the late afternoon and early evening.  This caused mostly minor wind damage from fallen trees. ||Another lull followed during the late evening.  However, in the humid air, more thunderstorms formed in southeast Ohio and moved into southern West Virginia after midnight, for the early morning hours on the 14th.  The axis of heaviest rain went southeast from Mason and Cabell Counties on through Putnam, Lincoln, southwestern Kanawha, and into Boone Counties.  The 24 hour rain totals of 2 to 3.5 inches were measured.   For example, a spotter in Hurricane had 3.3 inches of rain.  The cooperative observer at Madison had 2.65 inches of rain.  The automatic gauge at Hager Cemetery in Lincoln County measured 2.36 inches of rain.  Flash flooding was common.  ||Finally, more thunderstorms formed in northern Ohio ahead of a cold front and mid level disturbance during the midday and early afternoon on the 14th.  These storms formed into a squall line and moved southeast, through West Virginia during the late afternoon and early evening.  Rain amounts of .5 to 1 in an hour were enough to cause a few minor flooding problems again over the waterlogged counties in the south.  Yet, the main effect was strong winds on the leading edge of the squall line.  This caused electrical power outages.||In the aftermath, a gubernatorial state of emergency was declared for a few counties.  Eventually, a federal disaster was declared by President Obama for the period July 10th through the 14th.  It included 8 counties in West Virginia.  See FEMA disaster 4236 for more information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown across Route 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was creeping southeast through the upper Ohio River Valley.  Late at night, thunderstorms formed in portions of north central West Virginia and streaked quickly northeast.
A cold front was creeping southeast through the upper Ohio River Valley.  Late at night, thunderstorms formed in portions of north central West Virginia and streaked quickly northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were blown down along Route 119.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was creeping southeast through the upper Ohio River Valley.  Late at night, thunderstorms formed in portions of north central West Virginia and streaked quickly northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree limbs were blown down near the the intersection of Routes 20 and 119.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall developed across portions of the southern Appalachian mountains as clearing ahead of a slow-moving surface low over the Cumberland Gap allowed instabilities to rise into the 2000 J/Kg range along with good upper divergence. Developing storms and cell mergers produced rainfall rates of 1.5���/hr with totals up to 3-4��� in isolated storms. A Flash Flood Warning was issued for Mercer County at 737 PM EDT where radar had showed 1 to 2 inches of rain had fallen with more expected. Total rainfall amounts reached 2.5 to 3 inches in a 3-hour period ending around 02z (10 PM on the 5th) over parts of western Mercer County which produced substantial flash flooding and debris flows in several locations. An IFLOWS rain gage at Bramwell, WV recorded  3.47��� in a 3-hour period. This amount/duration was right around the 200-year annual recurrence interval (ARI) per NOAA Atlas 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 460 was closed due to flooding in Bluefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a large cluster of showers and thunderstorms, new storms formed over western Kanawha County during the evening hours.  These storms drifted north during the night, producing over 2 inches of rain in less than 3 hours.  Since the ground was initially dry, only minor flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads flooded and briefly closed.  This included Route 62.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a large cluster of showers and thunderstorms, new storms formed over western Kanawha County during the evening hours.  These storms drifted north during the night, producing over 2 inches of rain in less than 3 hours.  Since the ground was initially dry, only minor flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams and runs overflowed onto roads.  County highway crews cleared debris and replaced gravel.  Examples included the headwaters of Big Run along Route 31/3, plus along Buky Run and Route 8/5.      ||An automatic rain gauge near Cairo measured 2.3 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sank south across the region during the day, quickly returning during the overnight hours as a warm front. A shallow layer of cold air trapped in isolated spots of western Mineral County led to the development the freezing drizzle as the front moved north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS in Oakland reported freezing drizzle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was wedged against the eastern face of the Appalachians, while a deep low pressure system passed across the central Plains states.  The tight pressure gradient between the two features resulted in 60 to 65 knot southeasterly low level jet  along the central Appalachians.  Winds were particularly strong on the west side of the actual ridge lines, where downslope flow acted to draw the winds of the low level jet toward the surface, resulting in surface gusts to around 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southeasterly winds resulted in isolated reports of downed trees, as well as losses of power and telecommunications, across Mercer County.  At one point during the morning, at least 1,530 customers were reported to be without power.  One tree blown down was an 150-year old oak located on Bland Road in the town of Bluefield, which was well-known to local residents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture remained trapped at the surface as a warm front slowly lifted north, resulting in patches of dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MRB reported reduced visibilities of one quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the region produced light winds and clear skies, leading to patchy radiational dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MRB reported reduced visibilities of one quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region during the overnight hours. A pressure surge in the wake of the front produced gusty winds at elevations higher than 2000 ft.
Increased moisture from low pressure passing to the south combining with light winds under high pressure and mostly clear skies led to patchy dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MRB reported reduced visibilities of one quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of the 23rd, temperatures were in the 60s, while dew points were in the 50s.  A squall line raced up the Ohio River Valley, well out ahead of the surface cold front.  The fast moving showers and thunderstorms reached into the mid Ohio Valley around Parkersburg just before midnight. Wind gusts around 45 mph were common.  However, a brief EF0 tornado was declared in Wood County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF0 tornado occurred in the Larkmead area, south of downtown Parkersburg.  The most damage was along South Sixth Street. The National Weather Service survey team estimated the peak wind gusts at 70 mph. ||A few buildings lost portions of their roofs. A roof was completely removed from an old cement block building.  Its walls collapsed.  A small camper was destroyed. The man in the camper was not injured. Most of the trees that were knocked down had rotten trunks.  Twelve local residents had to be housed in local motels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convection system rode east and northeast out of Kentucky during Christmas morning.  Support for the rain was aided by a stalled out front in southern West Virginia, just south of Charleston, on into southeast Kentucky.  Abundant moisture was available. Dew points were near 60 degrees south of the front.  ||A band of moderate to heavy rain reached Huntington near 1000E. Meanwhile, new cells developed eastward into the Charleston vicinity at the same time. This enhanced rain lifted northeast along the Elk River and Interstate 79 corridor through northern Clay County and into southern Braxton County by midday.  The heavier rain finally weakened in the central mountain counties during the mid afternoon.  ||Rainfall of 1.5 to 2 inches was common along a narrow corridor in about 4 hours.  A few reports exceeded 2 inches.  A spotter in western Putnam County along the Charley Creek drainage measured 2.7 inches. Laurel Ridge in Clay County had 2.24 inches.  Queen Shoals and Big Chimney measured around 2.1 inches. Charleston's Yeager Airport had 1.94 inches of rain.  A spotter in South Charleston and the East End of Charleston both had 1.8 inches, while 1.7 inches fell at the National Weather Service Office at Southridge. Amounts around 1.8 inches were also measured by automatic gauges at Dingy, west of Frametown and at Bug Ridge, southeast of Sutton. ||The maximum 24 hour rain amounts from cooperative observer sites included 2.53 inches at Sutton, 2.37 inches at Saint Albans, and 2.25 inches at Gassaway.      ||The usual vulnerable poor drainage areas and streams overflowed and blocked roads. A few homes were flooded, others had basement flooding. Problems were compounded in urban areas and land disturbed by development.  A few landslides occurred where cuts were made in hillsides for roads.  ||In the aftermath, a strong rise occurred on the lower Elk River, but the crest remained below flood stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams caused minor flooding of roads, including Big Lynn Creek.

A few homes were flooded, others had basement flooding. Problems were compounded in urban areas and land disturbed by development.  A few landslides occurred where cuts were made in hillsides for roads.  ||In the aftermath, a strong rise occurred on the lower Elk River, but the crest remained below flood stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streams flooded and blocked roads.  This included Big Otter Creek blocking Route 16 and Laurel Fork flooding a portion of Route 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convection system rode east and northeast out of Kentucky during Christmas morning. Dew points were near 60 degrees south of the front.  ||A band of moderate to heavy rain reached Huntington near 1000E. Meanwhile, new cells developed eastward into the Charleston vicinity at the same time. This enhanced rain lifted northeast along the Elk River and Interstate 79 corridor through northern Clay County and into southern Braxton County by midday.  The heavier rain finally weakened in the central mountain counties during the mid afternoon.  ||Rainfall of 1.5 to 2 inches was common along a narrow corridor in about 4 hours.  A few reports exceeded 2 inches.  A spotter in western Putnam County along the Charley Creek drainage measured 2.7 inches. Laurel Ridge in Clay County had 2.24 inches.  Queen Shoals and Big Chimney measured around 2.1 inches. Charleston's Yeager Airport had 1.94 inches of rain. A few homes were flooded, others had basement flooding. Problems were compounded in urban areas and land disturbed by development.  A few landslides occurred where cuts were made in hillsides for roads.  ||In the aftermath, a strong rise occurred on the lower Elk River, but the crest remained below flood stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous small streams flooded roads around Strange Creek, Gassaway, Frametown, and Sutton.  One example was Little Otter Creek near Gassaway.  The headwaters of the Right Fork of Steer Creek flooded, including Joes Fork.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams in the extreme southern portion of the county flooded roads.  The headwaters of the Big Sandy Creek were involved.  This included the Middle Fork and Route 36 near Newton.  At least one modular home was flooded in Newton. This modular home was situated in an area local residents said floods about twice a year.  Apparently, the new owners were not aware of that. The family of 4 had left to visit relatives that morning, and most of their Christmas gifts were damaged.  Little Left Hand Run also flooded roads.  A landslide occurred along Interstate 79 near Amma where the hillside was cut away during the construction of that interstate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Poor drainage and stream flash flooding were common from the Saint Albans and Cross Lanes vicinity on east and northeast, through the north side of Charleston and into the Big Chimney and Clendenin vicinity.  ||In Saint Albans, water was over Stone Drive and Poplar Lane.  Washington Avenue and Spring Street in Saint Albans had water pond and seep into garages. The headwaters of Rocky Fork flooded New Goff Mountain and Big Tyler Roads with water getting into a few homes.  Flooding also occurred along Howard Fork and Route 62 in the vicinity of Cross Lanes. Woodward Branch flooded the intersection of Washington Street and Woodward Drive in Charleston. Lick Branch surrounded 3 homes near the lower end of South Ruffner Road in Charleston. Elk Two Mile flooded Rutledge Road off of Route 114.  Coopers Creek flooded roads in the Big Chimney area.||Yet, the most serious flash flooding was along Kanawha Two Mile in the Guthrie area.  In the headwaters, 12 people were evacuated from 6 homes along Lotus Drive.  The Rich Oil convenience store was flooded again along Route 21.  Falcon Drive was also affected. A few vehicles were flooded. ||After damage assessment, a total of 17 homes had major damage in the county, with the highest concentration along Kanawha Two Mile.  Other homes had less damage including water seeping into their basements. Small road slippages occurred in the aftermath of this heavy rain.  One example was along Wills Creek Road in the Elkview area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms formed overnight as a weak cold front sunk south of the Mason-Dixon. Theta-e ridge axis aligned over Central MD and south into VA allowed for instability to increase overnight, with storms becoming more numerous with a nearly easterly movement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on powerlines on Limestone Rd near Hollywood Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Saturated low levels from multiple rounds of heavy rain combining with overnight low level inversion led to widespread development of fog, with areas of dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: W99 reported reduced visibilities of one quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure built up over the region, resulting in light winds. Initially mostly cloudy skies kept temperatures moderate for the first half of the night. Clearing in the second half led to strong radiational cooling, dropping temperatures below freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple stations across the area reported temperatures below 32 degrees Fahrenheit.
